properties.title: 2025 Benin coup attempt
properties.description:
A small military faction calling itself the Comité Militaire pour la Refondation (CMR) failed to depose President Patrice Talon after a brief armed assault on the presidency.

Benin, once a bastion of democratic stability, faced a "polarised political scene" and "authoritarian drift" under President Talon. The move to create a second legislative chamber (Senate) and extend presidential terms stoked opposition fears of a permanent power grab. Simultaneously, the security environment in the north deteriorated significantly; 54 soldiers were killed in jihadist attacks in April 2025, leading to military grievances over "negligence towards soldiers killed on the front" and their families.

Early on December 7, gunfire erupted near the presidential residence in Cotonou. Eight-armed soldiers appeared on national television, led by Lieutenant-Colonel Pascal Tigri, claiming they had deposed the government to address the security failures against Al-Qaeda-linked groups. The attempt was quickly neutralized by loyalist forces with support from French intelligence and special forces. By the end of the day, the African Union and International IDEA issued strong condemnations, labeling the event a "grave violation" of constitutional order.

The failed putsch prompted a regional "contagion" alert, leading ECOWAS to consider military intervention to preserve civilian rule. President Talon utilized the aftermath to consolidate control, though international bodies urged him to respect term limits ahead of the 2026 elections. Analysts noted a possible "AES influence," as Niger’s junta had reportedly been informed of the operation in advance.

Citations:

Mesa, Aris. "Countering the Coup in Benin: A Jus ad Bellum Perspective." EJIL: Talk! (blog). December 2025.

"Benin: What Does the 7 December Coup Attempt Reveal?" IRIS France. December 2025.

International IDEA. "Statement on the Attempted Coup in Benin." December 8, 2025.

properties.title: 2025 Malagasy coup
properties.description:
Summary: An elite unit known as CAPSAT overthrew President Andry Rajoelina following weeks of youth-led "Gen Z" protests over economic mismanagement and power shortages.

Political Context: Madagascar suffered from "extreme poverty," with over 75% of its 30 million people living below the poverty line. Public anger boiled over in September 2025 due to chronic electricity and water cuts. President Rajoelina’s attempts to bypass the legislature and dissolve the National Assembly to block an impeachment vote served as the final catalyst for the military’s intervention.

Event Description: On October 12, the Army Personnel and Services Administration Corps (CAPSAT) seized the capital, Antananarivo, meeting little resistance as civilian police joined the demonstrators. The military unit’s leadership framed their action as answering, "the people’s calls." Rajoelina reportedly fled to Réunion island on a French military aircraft as the National Assembly proceeded to formally impeach him.

Aftermath: The African Union immediately suspended Madagascar’s membership, while the UN condemned the "unconstitutional change of power." CAPSAT announced a two-year transitional period under military leadership. Domestically, the Malagasy ariary plummeted, and fuel imports were disrupted, plunging the nation into severe economic uncertainty.

properties.title: 2024 Bolivia coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: On June 26, 2024, a faction of the Bolivian Army led by General Juan José Zúñiga attempted to seize power by occupying the government headquarters in La Paz before retreating hours later due to a lack of institutional support.

Political Context: Bolivia suffered from a severe economic crisis characterized by dwindling foreign reserves, fuel shortages, and high inflation. Simultaneously, the ruling Movement Towards Socialism (MAS) party split into two rival factions led by President Luis Arce and former President Evo Morales, who were on a collision course for the 2025 presidential candidacy.

Event Description: The military uprising targeted President Arce’s democratically elected government. In the mid-afternoon of June 26, 2024, soldiers and armored vehicles led by General Zúñiga occupied Plaza Murillo, the site of the country’s main legislative and executive offices.

An armored vehicle rammed into the main gates of the Palacio Quemado, allowing troops to breach the building. Within the Palace hallway, President Arce personally confronted Zúñiga, ordering the general to demobilize his forces and withdraw. Zúñiga claimed the military intended to "restore democracy" and release political prisoners like Jeanine Áñez. The situation reached a turning point when Arce appointed a new military high command, including General José Wilson Sánchez as the new army chief. Sánchez immediately ordered all mobilized personnel to return to their units. Lacking broader military backing and facing civilian mobilization in defense of democracy, the rebellious troops began their retreat from the plaza roughly three hours after the occupation began.

Aftermath: Authorities later arrested Zúñiga and former Navy commander Juan Arnez Salvador on charges of terrorism and armed uprising. The event intensified national polarization, with detractors alleging a self-coup ("autogolpe") intended to bolster Arce’s falling popularity, while the administration maintained it was a direct assault on constitutional order.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2023 Niger coup
properties.description:
Summary: On July 26, 2023, members of Niger’s presidential guard led by General Abdourahamane Tchiani detained President Mohamed Bazoum in the capital and seized executive power - citing deteriorating national security and governance.

Political Context: Niger had recently achieved its first peaceful, democratic transfer of power in 2021; however, the country faced chronic challenges including systemic poverty, corruption, and a surge in jihadist violence across the Sahel. These grievances, combined with rumored internal power struggles between Bazoum and military leadership, provided a pretext for the intervention.

Event Description: The primary target of the coup was President Mohamed Bazoum, who was blockaded and detained within his palace in Niamey by his own Presidential Guard. Initially, the uprising appeared to be a localized mutiny, and the broader military, the Nigerien Armed Forces (FAN), signaled readiness to defend the constitutional order. However, the situation reached a turning point when the military high command, led by General Abdou Sidikou Issa, declared their support for the mutineers to avoid "a deadly confrontation" between units. On state television, Colonel-Major Amadou Abdramane announced the formation of the National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland (CNSP). This announcement confirmed the dissolution of the constitution and the suspension of all government agencies. Key individuals involved in the consolidation of power included General Tchiani and General Moussa Salaou Barmou, a U.S.-trained special forces commander. Civilian protests in support of Bazoum were dispersed by gunfire from the Presidential Guard before the junta established full territorial control.

Aftermath: The coup resulted in the indefinite detention of President Bazoum and the imposition of severe ECOWAS regional sanctions that devastated Niger’s economy. Politically, the junta shifted away from Western security partnerships, expelling French and U.S. troops in favor of military cooperation with Russia and fellow juntas in Mali and Burkina Faso.

properties.title: 2023 Gabon coup
properties.description:
Summary: On August 30, 2023, senior military officers in Gabon seized power, placing President Ali Bongo Ondimba under house arrest, minutes after he declared victory in a disputed general election.

Political Context: The coup ended the 56-year dynastic rule of the Bongo family, which was plagued by allegations of systemic corruption, election fraud, and economic inequality. Despite Gabon's status as an upper middle-income oil producer, approximately one-third of the population lived in poverty with high youth unemployment.

Event Description: The primary target of the military intervention was President Ali Bongo Ondimba. Following the electoral authority's announcement of a victory for Bongo with 64.27% of the vote—results many observers deemed implausible—the military moved to annul the process. A group of high-ranking personnel, identifying as the Committee for the Transition and Restoration of Institutions (CTRI), appeared on national television to declare they were seizing power to defend peace and end the current regime. The decisive force was the Republican Guard, led by General Brice Oligui Nguema, Bongo’s cousin and former security chief.

The coup was largely nonviolent and received broad public support, with citizens celebrating in the streets of Libreville and Port-Gentil. Military units dissolved all state institutions, including the government, Senate, National Assembly, and Constitutional Court, and briefly closed the country's borders to consolidate control.

Aftermath: General Nguema was named transitional president, promising a return to civilian rule by 2025 while simultaneously consolidating power through a new constitution. Reports from 2025, however, note a landslide victory for Nguema, which some classify as a repackaging of military rule. Ali Bongo was released from house arrest into retirement, though his family faced detention for high treason. The country remains suspended from the African Union.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2023 Sudanese civil war
properties.description:
Summary: The 2023 conflict in Sudan began as a violent struggle for national control between two rival generals whose former alliance collapsed into a catastrophic civil war.

Political Context: Following the 2019 ousting of dictator Omar al-Bashir, a fragile power-sharing agreement between military and civilian leaders began to unravel. A military coup in October 2021 further stymied democratic progress. Tensions then escalated over the time it took for the integration of paramilitary forces into the national army.

Event Description: On April 15, 2023, fierce fighting erupted in Khartoum and quickly spread across the country. The conflict pitted the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF), led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), commanded by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, also known as Hemedti. This was not a typical coup against a civilian government but a struggle within the security sector itself, as the RSF sought to seize strategic sites including the airport and Presidential Palace. The target of the RSF’s initial offensive was the SAF's military command and the de facto leadership of al-Burhan. Neither side achieved a quick victory, as the SAF responded with airstrikes while the RSF leveraged its urban mobility. The failure to reach a rapid outcome transitioned the event from a power grab into a protracted civil war.

Aftermath: The war has created the world's largest displacement crisis, with over 13 million people forced from their homes. It has decimated the economy and caused widespread famine. Politically, the country remains fractured, with the SAF governing from Port Sudan while the RSF controls significant territory in the west and south.

properties.title: 2022 São Tomé and Príncipe coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: The 2022 coup attempt in São Tomé and Príncipe was a sudden overnight raid on military barracks that shattered the peace of one of Africa’s most stable democracies.

Political Context: São Tomé and Príncipe is a multiparty democracy with a history of five failed coup attempts since 1991. In September 2022, the Independent Democratic Action party won legislative elections, leading to a peaceful transfer of power. Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada was sworn in just weeks before the attack.

Event Description: Shortly after midnight on November 25, 2022, a small group of gunmen launched an attack on the national army headquarters in the capital city. The target of this coup attempt was the newly established government of Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada. Four civilians entered the barracks in search of weapons and abducted a sentry as a hostage. The group was led by Arlécio Costa, a former mercenary who had previously attempted a coup in 2003. Costa was reportedly disgruntled over the seizure of land by the previous administration. The military responded quickly, engaging in a battle that lasted until approximately 6:00 a.m. During the fighting, the army captured three of the intruders at the base. Authorities also arrested Delfim Neves, the former president of the National Assembly, on suspicion of supporting the plot. By dawn, the military had successfully thwarted the attempt and neutralized the attackers.

Aftermath: The event ended in tragedy when four suspects, including Arlécio Costa, died from torture while in military custody. While the government remains in power, the incident damaged the nation's democratic reputation. Several soldiers faced charges for the custodial deaths, and the army chief subsequently resigned.

Chicago Citations:

Associated Press. "Prime Minister: Coup Thwarted in Sao Tome and Principe." November 25, 2022.

properties.title: 2022 Burkinabè coup
properties.description:
Summary: On January 24, 2022, Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba led a military coup that ousted democratically elected President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré, citing the government's failure to contain a worsening Islamic insurgency.

Political Context: Burkina Faso's transition to democracy following the 2014 popular uprising that ended Blaise Compaoré's 27-year rule proved fragile. Though President Kaboré won peaceful elections in 2015 and again in 2020, rising jihadist violence that began in 2015 and killed thousands destabilized the country. By late 2021, widespread public dissatisfaction mounted as the military and civilian population suffered repeated defeats against al-Qaeda and Islamic State-affiliated groups, with the November 2021 Inata attack killing 49 soldiers. Kaboré's December 2021 military reshuffle and prime minister's resignation came too late to restore confidence.

Event Description: On January 23-24, gunfire erupted at the presidential residence and military barracks in Ouagadougou. Soldiers from multiple garrisons participated, coordinating attacks that overwhelmed state security. Military units detained President Kaboré at the Sangoulé Lamizana barracks. By afternoon on January 24, Captain Sidsore Kader Ouedraogo announced on state television that the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration (MPSR), led by Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Damiba, had seized power. The 41-year-old Damiba, recently promoted to command the capital's third military region, had military training in Paris and authored a book on West African counterterrorism. The coup dissolved parliament, suspended the constitution, and imposed a nationwide curfew. In military custody, Kaboré signed a resignation letter, and soldiers declared a promised return to civilian rule "within a reasonable time frame."

Aftermath: Damiba initially received public support and limited international engagement, though ECOWAS and the African Union condemned the coup and demanded constitutional restoration. However, Damiba's inability to improve security—violence increased 23 percent in his eight months in office—led to his own overthrow by Captain Ibrahim Traoré on September 30, 2022. The January coup thus marked Burkina Faso's continued instability and military dominance over democratic governance.

Chicago Citations:

Chin, John, Haleigh Bartos, and Aleksaundra Handrinos. "What Burkina Faso’s Tragic History Teaches Us." Journal of Democracy, October 2024.

Heo, Sora. "Vicious Cycles: Burkina Faso’s Coup Quandary." International Relations Review, January 3, 2023.

properties.title: 2022 Burkinabè coup by Traoré
properties.description:
Summary: On September 30, 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré, a 34-year-old artillery officer frustrated with military leadership, led a coup that ousted Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba, the junta leader who had himself seized power in January 2022, consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Political Context: Burkina Faso's political crisis intensified following the January 23-24, 2022, military coup that ousted President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré. Damiba, promising to restore security against a worsening Islamist insurgency, was initially welcomed by Burkinabe society frustrated by the civilian government's inability to contain jihadist violence. However, by September 2022, the security situation had deteriorated dramatically. Islamist groups controlled nearly 40 percent of Burkina Faso's territory; violence had surged, and military officers and frontline troops under Damiba's command complained that he prioritized his own political interests over defeating insurgents.

Event Description: On September 30, Traoré and disaffected military officers arrested Damiba and announced his deposition via state television, citing his failure to address the country's deteriorating security situation. They suspended the constitution, dissolved the transitional government and legislature, and imposed a nationwide curfew from 9 pm to 5 am. Traoré declared himself head of state and head of the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration. Initially, Damiba attempted to flee toward the French military base at Camp Kamboinsin to potentially mount a counter-coup; however, this claim is disputed by Traoré. On October 2, religious and community leaders mediated Damiba's formal resignation; he fled to exile in Togo after securing seven guarantees including protection for his allies and respect for the two-year ECOWAS transition timeline.

Aftermath: The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS, the African Union, the United Nations, France, and the United States all protested the unconstitutional seizure of power. However, Traoré promised to honor Damiba's ECOWAS commitment to restore civilian rule by July 2024. The coup set Burkina Faso on a trajectory of deepening military autocracy, expanding human rights abuses, and increasing isolation from international partners, reversing what remained of the country's democratic transition.

Chicago Citations:

Africa Center for Strategic Studies. "Understanding Burkina Faso's Latest Coup." September 30, 2022.

properties.title: 2021 Malian second coup
properties.description:
Summary: On May 24, 2021, Colonel Assimi Goïta, Mali's vice president and leader of a military coup nine months prior, orchestrated a second coup by arresting interim President Bah Ndaw and Prime Minister Moctar Ouane, effectively consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Political Context: Mali's political crisis intensified following the August 18, 2020 military coup that ousted President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta. International pressure from ECOWAS and the African Union forced the junta to establish a civilian-led transitional government, with Ndaw inaugurated as interim president on September 25, 2020, and Goïta as vice president. However, this civilian façade masked the military's persistent dominance over state affairs.

Event Description: On May 24, 2021, the interim government announced a cabinet reshuffle that removed two coup leaders—Colonel Sadio Camara (Defense Minister) and Colonel Modibo Kone (Security Minister)—from ministerial positions without consulting Goïta. That evening, military forces arrested both Ndaw and Ouane and detained them at the Soundiata Keïta military camp in Kati. On May 25, Goïta announced the coup on national television, claiming Ndaw and Ouane violated the transition charter and attempted to "sabotage" the transition. Both leaders resigned under detention on May 26 and were released on May 27. Following their release, Mali's Constitutional Court declared Goïta interim president, and he was sworn in on June 7. Choguel Kokalla Maïga, from the M5-RFP opposition coalition, was appointed prime minister in June to appease international actors.

Aftermath: The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS suspended Mali and imposed sanctions on transitional leaders. The African Union, United Nations, France, and United States all demanded democratic restoration. Mali's election timeline collapsed; instead of elections in February 2022, the junta extended the transition to 2026, definitively reversing the democratic experiment and establishing military rule as the permanent governing structure.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2021 Guinean coup
properties.description:
Summary: Military special forces seized power in Guinea on September 5, 2021, arresting President Alpha Condé and dissolving the nation's democratic institutions.

Political Context: Alpha Condé became Guinea's first democratically elected president in 2010 but grew increasingly authoritarian. In 2020, he oversaw a controversial constitutional amendment to bypass term limits. His third-term re-election was marred by violent state repression, widespread poverty, and allegations of rampant government corruption.

Event Description: On the morning of September 5, 2021, an elite military unit known as the Special Forces Group launched an assault in the capital city of Conakry. Led by Colonel Mamadi Doumbouya, a former French legionnaire, the unit targeted the Presidential Palace in the Kaloum neighborhood. Heavy gunfire broke out as the special forces moved to arrest the target of the coup, 83-year-old President Alpha Condé. During the brief but intense combat, between eight and twenty military members were killed. The special forces successfully detained Condé, later circulating images of the barefoot and disheveled leader in their custody. Doumbouya quickly appeared on state television draped in a national flag to announce the takeover. He declared that the military had seized power to end political personalization and address economic mismanagement.

Aftermath: Colonel Doumbouya was sworn in as interim president and established the National Committee for Reunification and Development to oversee the government. President Condé remained under house arrest without charge. International bodies including ECOWAS and the African Union suspended Guinea's membership and demanded a return to civilian rule.

properties.title: 2021 Chadian military succession coup
properties.description:
Summary: The sudden battlefield death of President Idriss Déby in April 2021 triggered an immediate military takeover in Chad, where a junta led by his son bypassed constitutional succession to seize control.

Political Context: Idriss Déby ruled Chad for thirty years after seizing power in 1990. His tenure was marked by autocratic control, frequent rebel incursions, and a 2021 election widely viewed as fraudulent. Despite domestic repression, he remained a vital Western ally in regional counter-terrorism efforts.

Event Description: On April 20, 2021, the Chadian army announced that President Idriss Déby had died from injuries sustained during a visit to the front lines while fighting FACT rebels from Lybia. Under the 2020 constitution, power should have transferred to the President of the National Assembly. Instead, a group of fifteen army generals dissolved the government and parliament to form a Transitional Military Council (CMT). This junta named the president’s son, General Mahamat Idriss Déby, as the new leader. The target of this extra-constitutional seizure was the existing legislative order. The military immediately suspended the constitution, closed borders, and imposed a nationwide curfew to consolidate their authority. International allies like France endorsed the new leadership despite its unconstitutional nature, citing the need for regional stability.

Aftermath: The takeover led to a prolonged eighteen-month transition that was later extended by another twenty-four months. The CMT faced domestic protests that were met with violent state repression and numerous arrests. Politically, the event ensured the Déby family maintained its grip on power despite promises of civilian rule.

properties.title: 2021 Sudanese coup attempt by al-Bashir loyalists
properties.description:
Summary: On September 21, 2021, al-Bashir loyalists and military officers from Sudan's armored divisions staged a failed coup attempt against the Sovereignty Council led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, seeking to reverse Sudan's 2019 democratic transition and prevent their former leader's extradition to the International Criminal Court.

Political Context: Sudan's political transition from 30-year dictator Omar al-Bashir's 2019 overthrow proved deeply fragile. The August 2019 power-sharing arrangement established a Sovereignty Council (5 military, 5 civilian members, plus 1 agreed-upon member) with al-Burhan as chair, while economist Abdalla Hamdok headed a civilian transitional government. However, the military resisted civilian-led security sector reform, ICC referrals of al-Bashir, and investigations into the 2019 Khartoum massacre that implicated officers in murder and sexual violence. Economic deterioration, inflation, and government ineffectiveness further strained the tenuous civil-military partnership.

Event Description: On September 21, dawn attacks began when Major General Abdel-Baqi Bakrawi led approximately 40+ officers and soldiers from the armored divisions to seize the Sovereignty Council, military headquarters, and state television in Omdurman. Officers negotiated surrender inside a weapons depot after loyal military forces repelled them. By day's end, more than 40 military and civilian al-Bashir supporters faced arrest. Prime Minister Hamdok condemned the "orchestrated coup by factions inside and outside the armed forces" as extending al-Bashir's legacy of attempting to abort the transition. The UN Secretary-General condemned the coup attempt and warned it could derail Sudan's political progress.

Aftermath: The failed September 21 coup functioned as what analysts termed a "trial balloon"—revealing vulnerabilities in the transition that General al-Burhan exploited just 34 days later with his successful October 25, 2021 coup, which dissolved civilian government entirely and reversed the democratic gains of 2019.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2021 Sudanese coup
properties.description:
Summary: On October 25, 2021, General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, chairman of Sudan's Sovereignty Council, orchestrated a military coup that dissolved the transitional civilian-led government and detained Prime Minister Abdalla Hamdok, reversing Sudan's democratic transition just months before the military was scheduled to hand power to civilian leadership.

Political Context: Sudan's August 2019 power-sharing arrangement between military and civilian leaders proved unsustainable. Under a 39-month transitional framework, the military would govern the first 21 months before transferring the Sovereignty Council chairmanship to civilian representatives in November 2021. However, al-Burhan and military officers resisted civilian-led security sector reform, international pressure to refer ousted dictator Omar al-Bashir to the International Criminal Court, and investigations into military responsibility for the 2019 Khartoum massacre that killed dozens during protests. Economic deterioration, inflation reaching 200 percent, unemployment, and food insecurity created public discontent that Prime Minister Hamdok's civilian government, composed of the diverse Forces of Freedom and Change coalition, could not adequately address. The September 21 failed coup attempt by al-Bashir loyalists created pretext for military intervention.

Event Description: On October 25, military forces arrested Hamdok and cabinet members, dissolved the Sovereignty Council and transitional government, suspended constitutional articles granting civilians power, and declared a state of emergency. Al-Burhan announced the coup as necessary to prevent "civil war" and preserve national unity. Hamdok, initially held at al-Burhan's residence, was later placed under house arrest. Hundreds of thousands of pro-democracy protesters subsequently took to Khartoum streets; security forces killed at least eight and injured over 140 demonstrators in response.

Aftermath: The African Union suspended Sudan's membership; ECOWAS, the United Nations, United States, and European Union condemned the coup and withheld aid. On November 21, 2021, after international mediation, Hamdok and al-Burhan signed a power-sharing agreement that temporarily reinstated Hamdok as prime minister before his resignation in January 2022, leaving military rule consolidated.

Chicago Citations:

Arab Center Washington DC. "Sudan Coup: The Regional Interference behind a Faltering Transition." Arab Center Washington DC, December 11, 2021.

Middle East Policy Council. "Sudanese Government Instability and Military Coup." Middle East Policy Council, March 20, 2024.

"Sudan coup explained: The military has taken over in Sudan. Here's what happened." CNN, October 26, 2021.

"Sudan coup 2021: Who is Abdel Fattah al-Burhan?" Middle East Eye, October 26, 2021.

Human Rights Watch. "Sudan: Military Takeover Threatens Rights." Human Rights Watch, October 26, 2021.

properties.title: 2021 Myanmar coup
properties.description:
Summary: On February 1, 2021, Myanmar's military, known as the Tatmadaw, staged a coup that detained State Counsellor Aung San Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and numerous other civilian leaders, ending the country's fragile decade-long democratic transition and returning Myanmar to full military rule.

Political Context: Myanmar's tentative democracy emerged from 49 years of military rule after 2011, when elections held in 2015 brought Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy (NLD) to power. Though a constitutional restriction prevented Suu Kyi from serving as president, she became State Counsellor, wielding de facto governmental authority. However, the 2008 military-drafted constitution preserved military dominance through appointment of 25 percent of parliamentary seats and veto power over amendments. Tensions escalated after the November 2020 elections delivered the NLD a landslide victory (396 of 476 seats), while the military's proxy Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) won only 33 seats—threatening Senior General Min Aung Hlaing's planned presidency-in-retirement. The military alleged, without evidence, that the elections involved "terrible fraud" and more than 10.5 million cases of potential irregularities.

Event Description: In early morning raids on February 1, military forces arrested Suu Kyi, Win Myint, NLD lawmakers, cabinet members, and civil society activists nationwide. The military blocked internet and communications, then announced a one-year "state of emergency" citing constitutional provisions allowing military control during national emergencies. Vice President Myint Swe, a military appointee, became acting president and transferred authority to Commander-in-Chief Min Aung Hlaing. The military promised new elections after one year, though independent analysts characterized the coup as entirely preemptive and constitutionally groundless.

Aftermath: International condemnation was swift and overwhelming. The United States, United Kingdom, France, Australia, and European Union condemned the coup. The Myanmar ambassador to the UN, Kyaw Moe Tun, publicly denounced the military before his dismissal. Massive pro-democracy protests erupted nationwide, with tens of thousands participating in civil disobedience; security forces responded with deadly force, establishing a militarized state that persists today.

Chicago Citations:

"2021 Myanmar coup d'état." Wikipedia, 2 weeks ago.

Council on Foreign Relations. "Myanmar's Troubled History: Coups, Military Rule, and Ethnic Conflict." CFR, January 31, 2022.

Human Rights Watch. "Myanmar: Military Coup Kills Fragile Democracy." Human Rights Watch, February 1, 2021.

"Myanmar coup explained: Why Aung San Suu Kyi was detained by the military." CNN, March 1, 2021.

"Myanmar's military stages coup d'etat: Live news." Al Jazeera, February 1-9, 2021.

properties.title: 2020 Malian coup
properties.description:
Summary: On August 18, 2020, junior and mid-ranking military officers led by Colonel Assimi Goïta staged a coup that arrested President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta and Prime Minister Boubou Cissé, overthrowing Mali's democratic government after three months of mass protests against corruption, economic failure, and the government's inability to combat jihadist insurgency.

Political Context: Mali's democracy fragmented after disputed parliamentary elections in March 2020. The Constitutional Court overturned results in several constituencies, sparking post-electoral violence and accusations of electoral fraud. Beginning June 5, the M5-RFP opposition movement mobilized massive demonstrations against President Keïta's corruption, mismanagement, and failure to contain extremist violence in the north and center. Despite initial concessions—appointing Constitutional Court judges, releasing detained opposition figures, pressuring his son's resignation—Keïta refused to step down. By August, tens of thousands of Malians continued demanding his resignation, civilian deaths from security force crackdowns reached 11, and economic collapse threatened millions facing extreme poverty.

Event Description: On August 18, soldiers initiated a mutiny at the Kati military base outside Bamako, an argument over promotions serving as the final spark for long-building tensions. Military units stormed the capital, arresting Keïta and Cissé. By midnight, Keïta announced his resignation to avoid bloodshed, and the government dissolved. Colonel Assimi Goïta declared himself leader of the National Committee for the Salvation of the People (CNSP), a military junta that claimed temporary stewardship to restore stability and hold elections.

Aftermath: International condemnation followed immediately. ECOWAS, the African Union, United Nations, United States, France, and European Union condemned the coup as unconstitutional. Yet initial popular relief overshadowed international opposition; Malians had exhausted hopes for Keïta's reform. The CNSP promised elections within nine months (later extended to 18 months), established a transitional government, but nine months later staged a second coup in May 2021, revealing the military's reluctance to surrender power—a pattern defining Mali's post-2012 trajectory.

Chicago Citations:

"2020 Malian coup d'état." Wikipedia, 1 month ago.

Council on Foreign Relations. "The Legacy of Military Governance in Mali." Africa Center, September 7, 2023.

"Five Things to Know About Mali's Coup." United States Institute of Peace, October 27, 2021.

"Mali's President resigns after he was arrested in a military coup." CNN, August 19, 2020.

properties.title: 2019 Gabon coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: A small group of junior soldiers seized Gabon's state broadcaster in January 2019 to oust the long-ruling Bongo dynasty, only to be crushed by security forces within hours.

Political Context: The Bongo family has dominated Gabon since 1967. President Ali Bongo Ondimba took office in 2009, but his 2016 reelection was marred by violence and allegations of fraud. By late 2018, Bongo’s prolonged absence following a stroke in Saudi Arabia created intense political uncertainty.

Event Description: Early on January 7, 2019, several soldiers led by Lieutenant Kelly Ondo Obiang seized the state broadcasting building in Libreville. Obiang, a commander in the Republican Guard, led a group calling itself the Patriotic Movement of the Defense and Security Forces of Gabon. The mutineers targeted the government of the ailing President Bongo, declaring him unfit to lead and announcing the creation of a "restoration council" to oversee a democratic transition. In a broadcasted video, Obiang urged citizens to rise up and take control of the streets to support the takeover. Some junior members of the Republican Guard, an elite unit meant to protect the presidency, participated in the plot. The government responded by cutting national radio and internet services while security forces moved to retake the building. Scattered gunfire broke out as loyalist troops confronted the small band of conspirators before their outcome was finalized.

Aftermath: Security forces retook the station by mid-morning, killing two mutineers and arresting several others, including Obiang. The incident prompted the African Union to strongly condemn the unconstitutional attempt to seize power. Ultimately, the failed coup underscored the regime's vulnerability during Bongo's recovery and led to increased political tension.

Chicago Citations:

Chappell, Bill. "Gabon Says Coup Attempt Is 'Under Control' After Soldiers Seized Radio Station." NPR, January 7, 2019.

Freedom House. "Gabon: Freedom in the World 2020 Country Report." 2020.

Husted, Tomas F., and Alexis Arieff. "Failed Coup Attempt in Gabon." Congressional Research Service, January 9, 2019.

United States Department of State. "Gabon 2019 Human Rights Report." Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights and Labor, 2020.

properties.title: 2019 Sudanese military coup
properties.description:
Summary: A joint operation by the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) ended the 30-year rule of Omar al-Bashir after months of mass pro-democracy protests.

Political Context: Sudan faced "thirty years of autocratic and kleptocratic rule" that decimated state institutions. The economy collapsed after the 2011 loss of oil revenue to South Sudan, triggering hyperinflation and the "December Revolution". Bashir’s inner circle eventually realized that violent repression was alienating the business elite and risking a civil-military split.

Event Description: On April 11, the military high command arrested Bashir and established the Transitional Military Council (TMC), led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan. The RSF, led by "Hemedti" Dagalo, initially tasked with protecting Bashir, switched sides to join the SAF in the takeover. Despite the coup, protesters remained in the streets, demanding a civilian-led transition, which eventually led to a shaky power-sharing agreement in August 2019.

Aftermath: The coup initiated a tumultuous transition marked by a Sovereignty Council shared between civilians and generals. However, the military retained a veto over key ministries. This power struggle ultimately culminated in the October 2021 coup and the subsequent 2023 civil war between the SAF and RSF.

Chicago Citations:

"Sudan: Military coup of 2018/2019." Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada, 2020.

"Civil War in Sudan | Global Conflict Tracker." Council on Foreign Relations, 2026.

properties.title: 2017 Zimbabwean coup
properties.description:
Summary: The Zimbabwe Defence Forces (ZDF) launched "Operation Restore Legacy" to oust 93-year-old Robert Mugabe and prevent the succession of his wife, Grace Mugabe.

Political Context: The coup was triggered by a "factional struggle" between the G40 faction (allied with Grace Mugabe) and the Lacoste faction (allied with Emmerson Mnangagwa). Tensions reached a breaking point when Mugabe dismissed Mnangagwa as Vice-President on November 6. The military, identifying itself as the "guardian of the liberation struggle," viewed the rise of Grace Mugabe as a threat to their institutional and economic privileges.

Event Description: On the night of November 14, tanks entered Harare, and soldiers seized the state broadcaster (ZBC). Major General S.B. Moyo appeared on television, famously denying it was a coup and claiming they were only targeting "criminals" around the President. Mugabe was placed under house arrest. After a week of political maneuvers and mass street celebrations, Mugabe resigned on November 21 to avoid impeachment.

Aftermath: Emmerson Mnangagwa was inaugurated as President. While international actors initially "accepted" the transition, hoping for democratic reform, the coup actually "reversed the strengthening of accountability" and set Zimbabwe back toward authoritarianism. The military’s political entrenchment was solidified, leading to a deepened socioeconomic crisis.

Chicago Citations:

Tendi, Blessing-Miles. The Overthrow of Robert Mugabe. Oxford University Press, 2025.

"The Motivations and Dynamics of Zimbabwe's 2017 Military Coup." DPIR (Oxford Academic), 2019.

properties.title: 2016 Turkish coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: In the late hours of July 15, 2016, several thousand Turkish military members initiated a coup attempt against current sitting Turkish president Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Following reports of unauthorized military aircraft over Ankara, Prime Minister Binali Yildirim stated that members of the Turkish military were illegally attempting to seize power from the Turkish Government.

Political Context: Over the course of several hours, military units across the country mobilized to seize key sites such as the Bosphorus Bridge, Istanbul’s Taksim Square, and Turkish television and radio stations. Alongside these seizures, various Turkish officials were detained by conspirators, with General Hulusi Akar allegedly being forced to sign a coup declaration. Following these operations, state broadcasters were forced by coup members to spread a message stating that a faction of the Turkish military had seized power from the Turkish government, citing a claim of a deterioration of secular and democratic ideology within the Turkish government. In this broadcast, they stylized their forces as the Peace at Home Council.

Event Description: At the onset of the coup attempt, the Turkish head of state, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, was several hundred miles from either Istanbul or Ankara, vacationing in Marmaris. Upon news of the initiation of a coup attempt, Erdogan flew to Istanbul, immediately making a speech denouncing the attempt. As the evening of the 15th moved into the early hours of the 16th, several notable clashes occurred, helicopters bombed various government buildings including the Turkish Parliament, and coup-affiliated forces attempted to detain President Erdogan, believing him to still be located in Marmaris.

Aftermath: Despite initial success, the coup attempt rapidly lost momentum as loyalist forces moved to intercept their operations across the country. By the morning of July 16, the Turkish government had regained control over most key sites previously controlled by coup conspirators. Government officials subsequently declared that the coup attempt had failed, moving rapidly to eliminate remaining pockets of resistance and detaining thousands of soldiers.

In the aftermath of the 2016 coup attempt, President Erdogan moved rapidly to purge the Turkish government, military, and various public servants. Following a declaration of a state of emergency, thousands of soldiers, judiciary officials, educators, and citizens were detained, suspended, or dismissed from their positions.

Chicago Citations:

Arab News source as provided.

BBC News source as provided.

U.S. State Department OIG source as provided.

CNN Türk source as provided.

properties.title: 2015 Burkinabè RSP coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: On September 16-17, 2015, soldiers of the Regiment of Presidential Security (RSP), an elite military unit loyal to overthrown former President Blaise Compaoré, staged a coup that temporarily detained Interim President Michel Kafando and Prime Minister Isaac Zida, attempting to reverse electoral reforms that threatened the RSP's dissolution.

Political Context: Burkina Faso's democratic transition began when popular protests in October 2014 forced 27-year dictator Blaise Compaoré from power after he attempted to amend the constitution to extend his rule. An interim government led by Michel Kafando was established to oversee elections scheduled for October 11, 2015. However, the transitional charter barred Compaoré-era officials from electoral participation—a provision that threatened the RSP, a 1,300-member presidential guard unit that had been Compaoré's loyal instrument of state control. In June 2015, Prime Minister Isaac Zida publicly called for the RSP's dissolution; on September 14, a reconciliation commission officially recommended its disbandment and redeployment. The looming loss of institutional power and impunity motivated immediate military action.

Event Description: On September 16, RSP soldiers stormed a cabinet meeting, detaining Kafando, Prime Minister Zida, and several cabinet members. The next day, an RSP spokesman announced the dissolution of transitional authorities and declared General Gilbert Diendéré—Compaoré's former chief of staff and intelligence chief—as leader of a new junta called the National Council for Democracy. The coup was overwhelmingly rejected. Massive protests erupted across Ouagadougou; civilians built barricades while RSP soldiers fired live ammunition into neighborhoods. The "Citizen Broom" movement and civil society organizations mobilized popular resistance. International condemnation was immediate and unified: ECOWAS, the African Union, the United Nations, France, the United States, and the European Union all condemned the coup. By September 22, loyalist army forces moved on the capital without resistance. Diendéré announced his resignation, and Kafando was reinstalled as president. The failed coup led to the RSP's dissolution and preserved Burkina Faso's democratic election on October 29, 2015, which Roch Marc Christian Kaboré won decisively.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2014 Ukrainian Revolution of Dignity
properties.description:
Summary: On February 22, 2014, after three months of escalating mass protests and street violence in Kyiv's Independence Square (Maidan), Ukrainian parliament voted to remove President Viktor Yanukovych from office in response to his November 2013 decision to reject the European Union–Ukraine Association Agreement and instead align with Russia.

Political Context: Ukraine's 2013–2014 crisis originated when Yanukovych, after meeting Russian President Vladimir Putin on November 9, 2013, unexpectedly reversed his commitment to sign the EU association accord scheduled for November 29. Ukraine's parliament had overwhelmingly approved EU integration; Yanukovych's reversal catalyzed massive protests. Beginning November 21 with 1,500 youth activists, Euromaidan (Euro Square) protests grew to encompass hundreds of thousands demanding constitutional reform, EU integration, and Yanukovych's resignation. Protesters occupied Kyiv's central Maidan Nezalezhnosti (Independence Square), establishing camps and blocking government buildings.

Event Description: The government responded with escalating repression. On November 30, riot police violently dispersed protesters, injuring dozens. Opposition parties joined the movement; civil society organized legal aid and medical care. The government imposed repressive anti-protest laws on January 16, 2014. Violence climaxed on February 20–21, when security forces fired live ammunition into crowds, killing 48–100 protesters in what became the bloodiest days. These killings united opposition parties and triggered defections from Yanukovych's Party of Regions. On February 21, Yanukovych signed an agreement restoring the 2004 constitution and promising early elections, but hardliners rejected the compromise. On February 22, with parliament lacking quorum from defecting government MPs, opposition-controlled parliament voted to strip Yanukovych of office. He secretly fled Kyiv for Russia.

Aftermath: The parliament appointed interim Prime Minister Arseniy Yatsenyuk to lead a transitional government, released imprisoned opposition figures including Yulia Tymoshenko, restored the 2004 constitution, disbanded the Berkut riot police, and scheduled new elections. Petro Poroshenko won May 2014 presidential elections. Russia interpreted Yanukovych's ouster as Western intervention, immediately occupying Crimea and supporting separatists in Donetsk and Luhansk, initiating the Russo-Ukrainian War.

properties.title: 2014 Burkinabè uprising and military takeover
properties.description:
Summary: A mass popular uprising, dubbed the "Black African Spring," forced the resignation of President Blaise Compaoré after he attempted to extend his 27-year rule.

Political Context: Compaoré had ruled since the 1987 assassination of Thomas Sankara. His regime relied on a "big man deep state" consisting of elite military units, traditional chiefs, and business patrons. Tensions exploded when Compaoré proposed a constitutional amendment to allow him to seek re-election in 2015.

Event Description: On October 30, hundreds of thousands of protesters stormed and set fire to the National Assembly in Ouagadougou. The military, sensing the regime's collapse, announced the dissolution of the government. Compaoré fled to Ivory Coast on October 31. A power struggle immediately ensued within the military between General Honoré Traoré and Lieutenant Colonel Isaac Zida, with Zida eventually declaring himself head of state in a "coup-within-a-coup."

Aftermath: Under international pressure, Zida agreed to a civilian-led transitional government. Although elections in 2015 led to a brief democratic period, the dismantling of Compaoré's "security networks" left the country vulnerable to a jihadist insurgency. This instability eventually triggered two subsequent military coups in 2022.

properties.title: 2014 Lesotho coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: The Lesotho Defence Force (LDF) seized the police headquarters and the Prime Minister’s residence, forcing the leader to flee to South Africa.

Political Context: Lesotho’s first coalition government, led by Prime Minister Tom Thane, was paralyzed by "chronic instability" and internal friction. The crisis was triggered when Thabane attempted to fire the army commander, Lieutenant General Tlali Kamoli, who refused to step down. This move exposed a deep rift where the army backed the opposition and the police remained loyal to the Prime Minister.

Event Description: Early on August 30, LDF troops occupied key installations in Maseru and disarmed police stations, killing one officer. Thabane fled to South Africa, declaring the action a coup. The LDF denied a coup attempt, claiming they were conducting a pre-emptive strike to prevent police from arming political "fanatics". SADC quickly intervened, facilitating a mediated return for the Prime Minister under South African police protection.

Aftermath: The "failed" coup led to early elections in 2015, which failed to resolve the underlying civil-military tensions. General Kamoli’s continued influence remained a point of contention until his eventual retirement and arrest in 2017. The event highlighted Lesotho’s "weak institutional guardrails" against military interference.

Chicago Citations:

"The 2014 Lesotho Coup Attempt: Causes and Consequences." Journal of Southern African Studies, 2015.

properties.title: 2014 Thai coup
properties.description:
Summary: General Prayut Chan-o-cha led the Royal Thai Army in a takeover following months of anti-government protests, establishing the National Council for Peace and Order (NCPO).

Political Context: Thailand was locked in a "decade-long cycle of political polarization" between the "Red Shirts" (supporters of Thaksin Shinawatra) and the "Yellow Shirts" (royalist/elite opposition). After the Constitutional Court removed Prime Minister Yingluck Shinawatra for abuse of power, a political vacuum emerged. The military claimed intervention was necessary to restore order and prevent civil war.

Event Description: On May 20, the army declared martial law without consulting the government. Two days later, during a meeting with political rivals at the Army Club, General Prayut detained the attendees and announced the coup on national television. The constitution was suspended, and media censorship was immediately enforced. Unlike previous coups, the NCPO focused heavily on "social order" and long-term reform to "purify" the democratic system of corruption.

Aftermath: The junta ruled directly for five years, significantly restructuring the 2017 constitution to ensure permanent military influence through an appointed Senate. While some stability returned, the coup deepened the generational divide and led to the massive youth-led protests of 2020-2021.

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2013 Egyptian coup
properties.description:
Summary: In 2011, on the wave of anti-authoritarian ideology produced by the Arab Spring, mass protests occurred across the Arab World. Within Egypt, millions of protestors gathered across the country in protest against President Hosni Mubarak. Criticized for his usage of police to repress civil rights, widespread corruption, and low wages, Mubarak was called upon to resign. Following 18 days of mass protests across the nation, Mubarak officially resigned on February 11th, 2011, transferring his authority to the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces (SCAF).

Political Context: Following a period of transition in which the Egyptian military oversaw an interim government, Egypt held its first democratic elections in the nation’s history. Throughout several elections, the Islamist group known as the Muslim Brotherhood took power. Mohamed Morsi, the candidate of the Muslim Brotherhood’s Freedom and Justice Party, won the presidential election, with SCAF relinquishing their authority to him, Morsi took office June 30th, 2012. Immediately following his ascension to the presidency, Morsi enacted sweeping reforms including the reestablishment of the Egyptian Parliament, the redrafting of the Egyptian Constitution, and dismantled SCAF’s authority, naming a new defense minister and annulling previous constitutional amendments that restricted the president’s power. Beyond these reforms, Morsi quickly purged the Egyptian government and military, consolidating his power.

In November of 2012, stating that his intention was to protect the drafting of the new Egyptian Constitution from judicial interference, Morsi issued a declaration that greatly expanded his presidential powers, making any actions he took as the president immune from any legal prosecution. This action was widely unpopular, with much of the government and the electorate finding his actions to be reminiscent of the prior decades of authoritarianism. Following his statement, protests erupted across Egypt, calling for a reversal of the declaration and the dissolution of the Constituent Assembly, the body responsible for the drafting of the new constitution.

In the weeks after the onset of mass protests, Morsi took steps to alleviate tensions, declaring that the decree was temporary, the scope of it was limited to sovereign matters, and most of the actions taken by the president would be subject to review by the judiciary. As Morsi’s time in office approached a year, various organizations began to plan his removal from office. On the 28th of June, protests calling for Morsi’s removal began to escalate, attracting counter-protests from the pro-Morsi movement. By this time, Tamarod, a large anti-Morsi organization, asserted that they had collected several million signatures on a petition calling for the removal of Morsi.

Event Description: Two days later, on the 30th of June, millions of protestors took to the streets of Egypt to call for Morsi to step down as president. Both civilians and government officials took part in mass demonstrations, with the protests extending into the first of July. Tamarod eventually issued an ultimatum, Morsi had twenty-four hours to step down from power. It was at this point that the Egyptian military became involved within the political turmoil of the country. On Monday, July 1st, 2013, a spokesperson for the Egyptian military announced that the government of Egypt had forty-eight hours to meet and come to an agreement with the electorate of Egypt. Over the course of the next two days, the political turmoil inside of Egypt escalated significantly, with dozens of people dying in clashes at protests, numerous high-ranking government officials resigning, and the Egyptian military threatening to suspend the constitution.

As the deadline passed, Morsi publicly rejected the Egyptian military’s ultimatum, stating that he would act unilaterally to resolve the political turmoil. One day later, the Commander-in-Chief of the Egyptian Armed Forces, General Abdel Fattah el-Sisi, ordered the arrest of President Morsi, publicly declaring that there would be a new presidential election. Following this, military leadership appointed Adly Mansour, Chief Justice of the Supreme Constitutional Court of Egypt, as interim president.

Aftermath: Following the arrest of President Morsi, the Egyptian military suspended the constitution amidst widespread support from the opposition. While Morsi had been removed from power, violence continued across the country with hundreds of people dying in protests. On July 8th, 2013, amid a pro-Morsi protest, Egyptian security forces allegedly opened fire on protestors without provocation. In the aftermath of the clash, several dozen Egyptian citizens had been killed. Several weeks later, on the 14th of August, security forces raided two separate camps of pro-Morsi protestors in Cairo. In this clash, thousands of protestors were trapped within the area surrounding their camp and subsequently fired on by Egyptian forces. In the dispersal of these pro-Morsi organizations, at least several hundred people were killed, with international observers estimating that number could be as high as several thousand.

Following the events of the 2013 Egyptian coup, presidential elections were held. Former General el-Sisi, who previously held control of the Egyptian military and the individual responsible for the removal of President Morsi from power, announced his retirement from the military and candidacy for the presidency. Opposing el-Sisi was Hamdeen Sabahi, a politician. The result of the 2014 Egyptian presidential election was overwhelmingly in favor of el-Sisi. Observers of the election questioned the validity of the result, citing several months of military crackdowns preceding the election.

The outcome of the 2014 election nonetheless finalized the 2013 coup’s objective of removing Morsi from power and implementing a new presidential election. Following the victory of el-Sisi, Egypt once again transitioned from military rule to a civilian government. However, the conditions in which the election occurred undermined its legitimacy. In the buildup to the election, opposition groups were repressed, the Muslim Brotherhood was barred from running a candidate, and the parameters surrounding the election were seemingly altered to favor el-Sisi. Rather than culminating in the restoration of democracy within the nation, the outcome of the 2013 Egyptian Coup resulted in the establishment of a new authoritarian regime, marred by numerous crackdowns and mass arrests.

properties.title: 2012 Guinea-Bissau coup
properties.description:
Summary: Military leaders led by General Mamadu Ture Kuruma seized power during a presidential election cycle to prevent alleged Angolan military intervention.

Political Context: Guinea-Bissau faced chronic instability, with no president completing a term since the 1990s. Tensions spiked during the 2012 presidential election between Carlos Gomes Júnior and Kumba Ialá. The military deeply resented the presence of the 200-strong Angolan Military Mission (MISSANG), which they viewed as a "glorified bodyguard" for Gomes Júnior intended to marginalize the national army.

Event Description: On the evening of April 12, mutinous troops used RPGs and grenades to attack the residence of frontrunner Carlos Gomes Júnior and interim President Raimundo Pereira. Within hours, the military seized the state broadcaster and PAIGC headquarters, taking both leaders into custody. The "Military Command" justified the action as a pre-emptive strike against a secret deal between Gomes Júnior and Angola to "annihilate" the Bissau-Guinean armed forces. No significant resistance occurred from the police, who retreated under heavy fire. International bodies, including the UN and AU, immediately condemned the disruption of the democratic process.

Aftermath: Under ECOWAS mediation, the junta released the detained leaders in late April but refused to restore the previous government. A transitional government was established in May 2012, led by Manuel Serifo Nhamadjo, with elections postponed until 2014. The coup solidified the military's role as a political arbiter and stalled efforts to combat the country’s reputation as a "narco-state."

Chicago Citations:

properties.title: 2012 Malian coup
properties.description:
Summary: Captain Amadou Sanogo led a mutiny that overthrew President Amadou Toumani Touré, citing government failure to contain a Tuareg rebellion.

Political Context: By early 2012, Mali's reputation as a "model democracy" masked deep internal rot. In the north, the MNLA and Islamist groups launched a major insurgency. Southern soldiers, poorly equipped and facing high casualties, blamed President Touré’s "consensus" politics for their battlefield losses. Protests by soldiers' wives in Bamako further eroded the regime's legitimacy.

Event Description: The coup began as an impromptu mutiny at the Kati military barracks on March 21 after a failed visit by the Defense Minister. Led by middle-ranking officers under Captain Sanogo, the rebels stormed the presidential palace and seized the state broadcaster (ORTM). President Touré escaped and went into hiding, protected by loyalist "Red Beret" paratroopers. Sanogo declared the formation of the National Committee for the Restoration of Democracy and State (CNRDR), suspended the constitution, and closed borders. The military takeover was swift, meeting minimal resistance in the capital.

Aftermath: Ironically, the coup accelerated the northern collapse; within ten days, rebels captured Kidal, Gao, and Timbuktu. Facing ECOWAS sanctions, Sanogo agreed to hand power to a civilian transitional government under Dioncounda Traoré in April 2012. However, the junta remained the "de facto" power in Bamako, leading to years of continued instability and foreign military intervention (Operation Serval).

Chicago Citations:

"Mali's Unfolding Coup." Stratfor Worldview, March 22, 2012.

Clingendael Institute. "The Roots of Mali’s Conflict: The 2012 Crisis." 2015.

TRT World. "A timeline of Mali's recent political instability." 2024.

Human Rights Watch. "Mali: Soldiers Torture, Murdered Red Berets." July 2012.

Al Jazeera. "Mali’s military leaders 'repel counter coup'." May 1, 2012.

properties.title: 2012 Malian counter-coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: Loyalists of ousted President Touré, primarily from the elite "Red Beret" parachute regiment, failed to retake Bamako from the March coup leaders.

Political Context: Following the March 22 coup, the military was fractured between the "Green Berets" (Sanogo's junta) and the "Red Berets" (the presidential guard). Friction peaked as the junta attempted to arrest Red Beret leaders and consolidate control over elite units. The Red Berets also sought to secure Bamako airport to facilitate the arrival of an ECOWAS intervention force.

Event Description: On April 30, Red Berets led by Colonel Abidine Guindo launched simultaneous attacks on the state television building, the Kati military camp, and the international airport. Fierce fighting involving machine guns and RPGs lasted overnight. The Green Berets counter-attacked with BRDM-2 armored vehicles and superior numbers. By the morning of May 1, the junta had repelled the assault and overrun the Red Beret base at Djicoroni. Casualties were estimated at 14–30 dead, with many loyalist soldiers captured or fleeing the capital.

Aftermath: The failure of the counter-coup led to a brutal purge. Human Rights Watch documented the torture and extrajudicial execution of at least 21 Red Beret prisoners at the Kati camp. Captain Sanogo's grip on Bamako tightened, though he was eventually promoted to General and then arrested in 2013 for these specific abuses. The elite paratrooper unit was effectively disbanded.

Chicago Citations:

"2012 Malian counter-coup attempt." Wikipedia, accessed March 2, 2026.

The Guardian. "Malian junta battles counter-coup attempt." May 1, 2012.

Al Jazeera. "Mali's military leaders 'repel counter coup'." May 1, 2012.

Human Rights Watch. "Mali: 'Red Berets' Trial Marks Progress." November 29, 2016.

Amnesty International. "Mali: Five months of crisis." July 2012.

properties.title: 2012 Sudanese coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: Sudanese authorities arrested former spy chief Salah Gosh and several military officers for an alleged plot to overthrow President Omar al-Bashir.

Political Context: In 2012, Sudan faced a crippling economic crisis following the 2011 secession of South Sudan, which cost Khartoum 75% of its oil revenue. Internal dissent grew within the ruling National Congress Party (NCP) and the military. Reformist "Sa'ihoon" Islamists and senior officers were increasingly critical of Defense Minister Abdel-Rahim Hussein’s failure to stop Israeli airstrikes and border setbacks.

Event Description: On November 22, the National Intelligence and Security Service (NISS) announced it had "pre-emptively foiled" a coup at "the zero hour." Forces arrested 13 people, including former NISS Director Salah Gosh and Brigadier Mohammed Ibrahim (Wad Ibrahim), a popular veteran. No shots were fired, and no tanks moved into the streets. While the government claimed the group planned to seize key installations, some observers suspected the "plot" was a manufactured purge to eliminate influential rivals to President al-Bashir.

Aftermath: The arrests highlighted deep fissures within Sudan's Islamist establishment. While several officers were sentenced to prison terms, President al-Bashir issued pardons in early 2013 to maintain elite cohesion. Salah Gosh was released in July 2013 after charges were dropped for lack of evidence. The event presaged the eventual 2019 uprising that would finally end Bashir's three-decade rule.

Chicago Citations:

ISS Africa. "The attempted coup d'etat in Sudan." November 30, 2012.

Sudan Tribune. "Sudan’s ex-spy chief freed after coup charges dropped." July 10, 2013.

"2012 Sudanese coup attempt." Wikipedia, accessed March 2, 2026.

Al Jazeera. "Sudan frees ex-spy chief held over coup plot." July 10, 2013.

CNN. "Coup attempt disrupted, Sudanese government says." November 23, 2012.

properties.title: 2012 Maldivian transfer-of-power crisis
properties.description:
Summary: President Mohamed Nasheed resigned following a police mutiny and weeks of protests, later labeling the event a "coup d'état."

Political Context: The Maldives’ first democratically elected president, Mohamed Nasheed, faced a constitutional crisis after he ordered the military to arrest Chief Judge Abdulla Mohamed. This move sparked mass protests by opposition parties, who accused Nasheed of being "anti-Islamic." Economic grievances, including a sharp currency devaluation, further fueled public anger and eroded support among security forces.

Event Description: On February 7, several hundred police officers mutinied in the capital, Malé, after refusing to break up opposition rallies. They seized the state broadcaster and clashed with Maldives National Defence Force (MNDF) troops. By midday, mutineering police and soldiers surrounded the President’s office. Nasheed, claiming he was being held at "gunpoint" or faced a bloodbath, resigned on national television. Vice President Mohamed Waheed was immediately sworn in as president. The military denied it was a coup, calling it a "voluntary" resignation.

Aftermath: Nasheed’s supporters launched massive "street action" protests, leading to police brutality. A Commonwealth-backed Commission of National Inquiry (CoNI) concluded in August 2012 that the transfer of power was legal and constitutional. Nasheed’s party rejected the findings, and the Maldives remained politically polarized, eventually leading to Nasheed's controversial imprisonment on "terrorism" charges in 2015.

Chicago Citations:

"The Fall of the Island President." Time Magazine, February 7, 2012.

Al Jazeera. "Panel rules Maldives power transfer 'legal'." August 30, 2012.

Government of Maldives. "Address to the nation on the CONI report." August 30, 2012.

Commonwealth Secretariat. "Report of the Commission of National Inquiry." 2012.

BBC News. "Maldives: Mohamed Nasheed 'forced out in coup'." February 8, 2012.

properties.title: 2012 Papua New Guinean mutiny
properties.description:
Summary: Retired Colonel Yaura Sasa led a small group of soldiers to seize the military headquarters in an attempt to reinstate Michael Somare as Prime Minister.

Political Context: Papua New Guinea was gripped by a "two prime ministers" crisis. Peter O'Neill held power with parliamentary support, while Sir Michael Somare claimed the office based on a Supreme Court ruling. The country's institutions were split, with the police and civil service largely backing O'Neill, while Somare's faction attempted to use judicial and military leverage to regain control.

Event Description: In the early hours of January 26, approximately 20–30 soldiers loyal to Somare stormed the Murray Barracks in Port Moresby. They placed the head of the army, Brigadier-General Francis Agwi, under house arrest. Colonel Sasa declared himself the new commander and issued a seven-day ultimatum for O'Neill to reinstate Somare or face "further action." The mutiny was bloodless and small-scale, with most of the military remaining neutral or loyal to the O'Neill government.

Aftermath: The mutiny collapsed within 15 hours. Most of the mutineers surrendered after the government threatened them with treason charges. General Agwi was released, and Colonel Sasa was later arrested. Prime Minister O'Neill successfully consolidated power and proceeded to national elections in mid-2012, which ultimately resolved the political impasse in his favor.

Chicago Citations:

Al Jazeera. "Troop mutiny sparks Papua New Guinea crisis." January 26, 2012.

The Guardian. "Papua New Guinea military coup fails." January 26, 2012.

Reuters. "PNG mutiny over, soldiers surrender: PM." January 27, 2012.

Lowy Institute. "The PNG Mutiny: A Failed Gamble." 2012.

BBC News. "PNG mutiny leader Yaura Sasa arrested." January 29, 2012.

properties.title: 2011 Egyptian revolution and military takeover
properties.description:
Summary: On February 11, 2011, after eighteen days of massive popular protests that mobilized hundreds of thousands of Egyptians demanding "bread, freedom, and dignity," President Hosni Mubarak resigned and transferred power to the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces, ending his thirty-year authoritarian dictatorship but establishing military rule as the custodian of transition.

Political Context: Egypt's 2011 uprising emerged from decades of discontent under Mubarak's authoritarian regime (1981–2011), characterized by police brutality, widespread torture, endemic corruption, economic stagnation, and rigged elections. A state of emergency since 1981 had legalized censorship and suspended constitutional rights. Mubarak's plan to position his son Gamal as successor further inflamed public resentment. The Tunisian overthrow of President Ben Ali on January 14 catalyzed action; on January 25, youth-organized demonstrations erupted across Egypt, with Tahrir Square in Cairo becoming the symbolic epicenter. Demands included Mubarak's immediate resignation, constitutional reform, and democratic elections.

Event Description: The regime responded with escalating violence: security forces fired live ammunition into crowds; police stations burned; labor unions called nationwide strikes halting economic activity. The death toll reached at least 846, with over 6,400 injured. Despite Mubarak's February 1 concession to step down at term's end (September), protesters rejected compromise: the chant "We're not leaving, he is!" became their rallying cry. The military, initially ambiguous, gradually withdrew support from Mubarak. By February 10, Defense Minister Mohamed Hussein Tantawi held strategic meetings excluding Mubarak and Vice President Omar Suleiman.

Aftermath: On February 11, Suleiman announced Mubarak's resignation. The military assumed control via the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces, promising to manage the country's affairs. Celebrations erupted nationwide; Tahrir Square filled with jubilant crowds. However, the military's seizure of power rather than transfer to civilian revolutionaries proved consequential—it prevented revolutionary consolidation and enabled military entrenchment. Egypt held its first multi-candidate election in 2012, but the military coup of July 2013 against elected president Mohamed Morsi restored authoritarian rule under General Abdel Fattah el-Sisi.

Chicago Citations:

"2011 Egyptian revolution." Wikipedia, 5 Feb. 2026.

"Egyptian Crisis (2011–2014)." Wikipedia, 4 Jan. 2026.

"Egypt Uprising of 2011: Causes, Impact & Legacy." Britannica, 4 Feb. 2026.

al-Anani, Khalil. "Ten Years Later: Reflections on Egypt's 2011 Uprising." Arab Center DC, 12 July 2024.
