properties.title: 2010 Guinea-Bissau military mutiny
properties.description:
Summary: Soldiers led by Deputy Chief of Staff Antonio Indjai detained the Prime Minister and the Army Chief of Staff, effectively seizing control of the military hierarchy.

Political Context: Guinea-Bissau had been plagued by "recurrent instability" following the 2009 assassinations of President Vieira and General Na Waie. The country’s "militarized democratization" meant that political actors and military factions were deeply entwined, often subverting democratic order to protect personal or factional interests. The 2010 mutiny was triggered by internal military rivalries and the government's attempt to investigate past political killings.

Event Description: On April 1, soldiers seized Prime Minister Carlos Gomes Júnior and the Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces, Admiral José Zamora Induta. Antonio Indjai, who led the uprising, threatened to kill the Prime Minister if pro-government protests continued. While Gomes Júnior was eventually released from house arrest, Induta remained detained. The mutiny was bloodless but resulted in a "socio-legal fiction" where the civilian government remained in office while the military held the true power.

Aftermath: The event solidified the military's role as the "real power" in Guinea-Bissau. Despite international condemnation, Indjai was later promoted to Chief of Staff. This mutiny set the stage for the subsequent 2012 coup, further entrenching the country's status as a "fragile state" highly dependent on international aid.

Chicago Citations:

"Guinea-Bissau: 30 years of militarized democratization (1991–2021)." Frontiers in Political Science (Academic Journal), May 2023.

"Civil–military relations and political order in Guinea-Bissau." The Journal of Modern African Studies (Academic Journal), May 2012.

"Introduction: Guinea-Bissau Today—The Irrelevance of the State and the Permanence of Change." ResearchGate (Academic Article), 2009.

properties.title: 2010 Niger coup
properties.description:
Summary: A military junta calling itself the CSRD overthrew President Mamadou Tandja after he attempted to illegally extend his term through constitutional manipulation.

Political Context: President Tandja had sparked a constitutional crisis in 2009 by dissolving parliament and the Constitutional Court to clear the way for a referendum that would allow him to stay in power beyond his two-term limit. This "autocratic overreach" alienated both the political opposition and the military leadership, who viewed his actions as a violation of the constitutional order they were sworn to protect.

Event Description: During a cabinet meeting at the presidential palace, soldiers led by Major Salou Djibo stormed the building using armored vehicles and heavy weaponry. After a brief firefight that resulted in several casualties (estimated between 3 and 10 deaths), President Tandja and his ministers were detained. The junta, the Supreme Council for the Restoration of Democracy (CSRD), immediately suspended the constitution and dissolved all state institutions, claiming their goal was to make Niger an "anchor of stability" once more.

Aftermath: Unlike many regional takeovers, the 2010 coup was widely seen as a "pro-democratic" intervention. The CSRD organized a transition that led to a new constitution and free elections in 2011, won by Mahamadou Issoufou. However, the precedent of military intervention remained a long-term risk for Nigerien democracy.

Chicago Citations:

"From Anchor of Stability to Crisis Hotspot: Background and Consequences of the Military Coup in Niger." SWP Berlin (Academic/Policy Report), December 2023.

"What underlies the coup in Niger?" Brookings Institution (NGO/Think Tank), August 2023.

"Guinea: The Causes and Consequences of West Africa's Latest Coup." CSIS (Think Tank/NGO), September 2021.

properties.title: 2009 Honduran coup
properties.description:
Summary: The military detained President Manuel Zelaya and forced him into exile following a bitter dispute over a proposed constitutional referendum.

Political Context: President Zelaya had become increasingly polarized from other government branches due to his plan to hold a non-binding referendum on whether to convene a National Constituent Assembly to rewrite the constitution. The Supreme Court and Congress ruled the referendum illegal, viewing it as a move to eliminate presidential term limits—a perception fueled by Zelaya's alliance with Venezuela’s Hugo Chávez.

Event Description: On the morning of June 28, the day the referendum was scheduled, soldiers raided Zelaya’s residence and flew him to Costa Rica. Congress then appointed Speaker Roberto Micheletti as interim president, claiming a "constitutional succession". The international community, including the UN and OAS, universally condemned the ouster as an "unconstitutional interruption of the democratic order". Protests followed, which were met with a "brutal response" by interim authorities, including documented human rights violations.

Aftermath: Honduras was suspended from the OAS and faced significant economic sanctions. Despite the domestic and international outcry, the Micheletti government remained in power until the November 2009 elections, which saw Porfirio Lobo Sosa elected. The coup left a "menacing precedent" for democracy in the hemisphere.

Chicago Citations:

"Honduran Political Crisis, June 2009-January 2010." EveryCRSReport (IGO/Gov Report), February 2010.

"Honduras: Constitutional Crisis and Coup D'Etat (2009)." Physicians for Human Rights (NGO Report).

"Honduras: Coup d'Etat in Constitutional Clothing?" American Society of International Law (Academic Journal), 2009.

properties.title: 2008 Guinea-Bissau coup plot
properties.description:
Summary: Authorities arrested the head of the Navy, Rear Admiral José Americo Bubo Na Tchuto, for allegedly plotting to overthrow the government.

Political Context: The state in Guinea-Bissau was characterized by "institutional fragility" and "factionalism" within the elite. By 2008, the country was increasingly labeled a "narco-state" due to the involvement of high-ranking military officials in international drug trafficking. Internal rivalries between different branches of the military, particularly the Army and the Navy, were constant drivers of instability.

Event Description: On August 6, the government announced it had foiled a plot led by Admiral Na Tchuto to seize power. Na Tchuto was placed under house arrest but later managed to escape to neighboring Gambia. The "plot" was largely seen as part of the ongoing "privatisation of power and resources" among the Bissau-Guinean elite rather than an ideological struggle. No actual combat took place, as the arrest occurred before any military units could be mobilized.

Aftermath: The failure of the plot did not end the turmoil; just months later, in March 2009, both the President and the Army Chief of Staff were assassinated in a "political crisis" that shocked the international community. Na Tchuto eventually returned to the country and was later targeted by U.S. DEA stings for his role in drug trafficking.

Chicago Citations:

"Political Instability in Guinea-Bissau." Routledge (Academic Book), 2024.

"Guinea-Bissau - African Studies." Oxford Bibliographies (Academic Database), April 2018.

"Introduction: Guinea-Bissau Today—The Irrelevance of the State and the Permanence of Change." ResearchGate (Academic Article), 2009.

properties.title: 2008 Mauritanian coup
properties.description:
Summary: General Mohamed Ould Abdel Aziz led a military junta to oust President Sidi Ould Cheikh Abdallahi after the president attempted to fire senior military commanders.

Political Context: Sidi Ould Cheikh Abdallahi was the first civilian president ever elected in a multi-candidate election in Mauritania. His 15-month administration was marked by a "three-month political crisis" involving bitter disagreements with parliament and a series of terrorist attacks by AQIM. The military, which had a "strong presence" in Mauritanian politics since independence, felt threatened by Sidi's attempts at civilian control and his "inept approach" to counter-terrorism.

Event Description: The coup was directly precipitated by President Sidi’s decree to fire the army chief of staff and the head of the presidential guard, General Abdel Aziz. Within hours, soldiers seized the president and prime minister, took control of state media, and announced the formation of an 11-man military junta, the High State Council (HSC). The takeover was bloodless, meeting little immediate physical resistance from the fragmented civilian institutions.

Aftermath: Mauritania returned to its "usual pattern of military-dominated autocratic rule". While international pressure led to the Dakar Accord and new elections in July 2009, General Abdel Aziz won that vote, effectively legitimizing his seizure of power. His regime was later characterized by "security force impunity" and harsh treatment of detainees.

Chicago Citations:

"Mauritania 2010 Human Rights Report." U.S. State Department (IGO/Gov Report), April 2011.

"Countering Terrorism in an Unstable Democracy: Mauritania's Political Crisis." West Point CTC (Academic Journal), June 2010.

"Mauritania: Freedom in the World 2011." Freedom House (NGO Report), 2011.

properties.title: 2008 Guinean coup
properties.description:
Summary: Following the death of longtime dictator Lansana Conté, a military junta (CNDD) led by Captain Moussa Dadis Camara seized power to block the constitutional transition.

Political Context: President Conté had ruled Guinea since 1984, leaving a legacy of economic stagnation and a "bloated, indisciplined military". The constitution mandated that the President of the National Assembly should lead a 60-day transition. However, younger officers in the military resented the "old guard" and sought to end the "corrupt system" they blamed for the country's poverty.

Event Description: Hours after the official announcement of Conté’s death, Captain Dadis Camara appeared on state radio to announce the dissolution of the government and the constitution. The takeover was bloodless; the civilian government and senior military brass offered no resistance. Dadis Camara promised a "war on corruption" and initial popular support was high as citizens hoped for change.

Aftermath: The honeymoon ended when Camara hinted he would run for president. In September 2009, security forces massacred over 150 protesters in a Conakry stadium. Camara was later shot in an assassination attempt by his own aide and fled to exile, eventually leading to Guinea's first democratic elections in 2010.

Chicago Citations:

"Guinea: The 2008 Coup and its Consequences." African Affairs, 2010.

"Military Rule and the September 2009 Massacre in Guinea." Human Rights Quarterly, 2011.

International Crisis Group. "Guinea: Military Rule and Democratic Transition." 2009.

"The 2008 Guinea Coup: A Study in Military Succession." Journal of Modern African Studies, 2012.

United Nations Security Council. "Report of the International Commission of Inquiry on Guinea." 2009.

properties.title: 1991 Chadian coup attempt by Maldoum Bada Abbas
properties.description:
Summary: Security forces loyal to President Idriss Déby suppressed a coup attempt led by his former ally, Interior Minister Maldoum Bada Abbas.

Political Context: Idriss Déby had seized power in 1990 after ousting Hissène Habré. The new regime was a fragile coalition of ethnic factions from the Patriotic Salvation Movement (MPS). Maldoum Bada Abbas, representing the Hadjerai ethnic group, felt marginalized by Déby’s increasing reliance on his own Zaghawa kinsmen for sensitive security posts.

Event Description: On September 25, soldiers loyal to Maldoum Bada Abbas attempted to seize key military installations in N'Djamena. Heavy fighting broke out around the airport and the presidential palace. Déby’s forces, benefiting from French logistical advice, quickly regained control. Maldoum Bada Abbas was arrested, and dozens of his supporters were reportedly executed or disappeared.

Aftermath: The failure of the coup allowed Déby to consolidate power and purge the Hadjerai faction from the government. It signaled the beginning of a long period of "Zaghawa dominance" in Chadian politics and security. International human rights groups condemned the "extrajudicial reprisals" following the attempt.

properties.title: 2000 Fijian coup
properties.description:
Summary: Armed gunmen led by businessman George Speight stormed Parliament and held the government hostage for 56 days, demanding "Indigenous Fijian paramountcy".

Political Context: Tensions between Indigenous Fijians and Indo-Fijians spiked following the 1999 election of Mahendra Chaudhry, the country's first Indo-Fijian Prime Minister. Speight utilized "ethno-nationalist rhetoric" to channel grievances regarding land rights and political representation.

Event Description: On May 19, Speight and a unit of the army's elite Counter Revolutionary Warfare (CRW) unit took Chaudhry and his cabinet hostage. The military, under Commodore Frank Bainimarama, initially declared neutrality but eventually declared martial law to "restore order" and deposed the civilian President. After two months of negotiations, the hostages were released in exchange for immunity, but the military soon reneged and arrested Speight.

Aftermath: While the coup failed in its immediate aim to install Speight, it succeeded in removing Chaudhry’s government. An interim administration was formed, but the events of 2000 set the stage for Bainimarama’s own coup in 2006, creating a "culture of coups" that damaged Fiji's economy and international standing for decades.

"Fiji's 2000 Coup: The Role of the Military and the Church." Journal of Pacific History, 2001.

properties.title: 1999 Guinea-Bissau coup
properties.description:
Summary: After an 11-month civil war, the Military Junta led by General Ansumane Mané overthrew President João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira.

Political Context: The conflict began in June 1998 when Vieira fired Mané, accusing him of smuggling arms to rebels in Senegal’s Casamance region. The military rebelled, leading to a war that displaced 40% of the population. Despite several peace agreements brokered by ECOWAS (ECOMOG), the two factions remained in a "volatile stalemate".

Event Description: In May 1999, the Military Junta launched a final offensive against Vieira’s loyalist presidential guard. The junta seized the capital, forcing Vieira to take refuge in the Portuguese embassy. The intervention was characterized by the junta as a "liberation" from Vieira's 19-year "autocratic rule". Vieira eventually went into exile in Portugal, and the junta installed the Speaker of Parliament as interim president.

Aftermath: The 1999 coup failed to stabilize the country, as the military continued to operate as a "shadow government". Guinea-Bissau remained trapped in a cycle of instability, culminating in the assassination of both the President and Army Chief in 2009, largely fueled by the burgeoning trans-Atlantic drug trade.

Chicago Citations:

"The 1998-1999 Civil War in Guinea-Bissau." African Affairs, 2000.

"Military Intervention and State Collapse in Guinea-Bissau." Journal of Modern African Studies, 2001.

ECOWAS. "Report on the Conflict in Guinea-Bissau and the ECOMOG Mission." 1999.

properties.title: 1999 Nigerien presidential guard coup
properties.description:
Summary: The Presidential Guard assassinated President Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara at a military airport, leading to a transitional military junta that restored multi-party democracy.

Political Context: General Baré Maïnassara had originally seized power in a 1996 coup and subsequently won a highly disputed election. His administration was characterized by "authoritarian drift" and a worsening economic crisis, which led to widespread strikes and civil unrest. By early 1999, the political opposition and segments of the military were increasingly alienated by Baré's refusal to acknowledge local election losses.

Event Description: On April 9, 1999, as President Baré was preparing to board a helicopter at the Niamey airport, members of his own Presidential Guard opened fire with truck-mounted machine guns, killing him instantly. The military initially described the assassination as an "unfortunate accident" but quickly moved to dissolve the National Assembly. Major Daouda Malam Wanké, the commander of the Presidential Guard, was named head of the National Council for Reconciliation (CRN).

Aftermath: Despite the violent nature of the coup, the CRN was praised for its "unusually rapid" transition to civilian rule. Wanké honored his promise not to run for office; a new constitution was approved via referendum, and free elections were held in October 1999, won by Mamadou Tandja. This transition was viewed as a "corrective coup" that ended Baré's autocracy and laid the foundation for Niger's Fourth Republic.

Chicago Citations:

"Niger: A History of Military Interventions." Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Politics, 2021.

properties.title: 1999 Ivorian coup
properties.description:
Summary: General Robert Guéï led a Christmas Eve military uprising that overthrew President Henri Konan Bédié, marking the first coup in the country's post-independence history.

Political Context: Ivory Coast, long considered a "bastion of stability" in West Africa, suffered under the "Ivoirité" policy introduced by Bédié. This xenophobic ideology aimed to disqualify political rivals—most notably Alassane Ouattara—by questioning their nationality, which deeply polarized the country along ethnic and religious lines. Economic decline and unpaid wage arrears for soldiers provided the immediate spark for the military's intervention.

Event Description: The coup began as a mutiny by low-ranking soldiers demanding back pay on December 23. By December 24, General Robert Guéï, the former army chief who had been dismissed by Bédié, emerged as the leader of the movement. Rebel troops seized the international airport, the state broadcaster, and key bridges in Abidjan. President Bédié fled to a French military base and was subsequently exiled to Togo. Guéï announced the formation of the National Committee of Public Salvation (CNSP) and promised to "sweep the house clean" of corruption.

Aftermath: The coup shattered the myth of Ivorian exceptionalism and initiated a decade of "instability and civil conflict". Although Guéï promised a quick transition to democracy, his attempt to rig the 2000 elections led to a popular uprising that eventually brought Laurent Gbagbo to power. The tensions exacerbated by the 1999 coup directly contributed to the outbreak of the Ivorian Civil War in 2002.

Chicago Citations:

"Ivoirité and the Crisis of Citizenship in Ivory Coast." African Affairs, 2003.

properties.title: 1999 Comorian coup by Azali Assoumani
properties.description:
Summary: Colonel Azali Assoumani seized power in a bloodless coup to resolve a constitutional vacuum and address the ongoing secessionist crisis on Anjouan island.

Political Context: The Comoros archipelago faced "chronic instability" following the 1997 secessionist movement on the islands of Anjouan and Mohéli. The death of President Mohamed Taki Abdoulkarim in 1998 created a leadership vacuum, as the interim president, Tadjidine Ben Said Massounde, struggled to negotiate a power-sharing agreement known as the Antananarivo Accord. Military officers and many citizens feared that the civilian government’s inability to reach a settlement would lead to the total disintegration of the Federal Islamic Republic.

Event Description: On April 30, 1999, Colonel Azali Assoumani, the Army Chief of Staff, led a swift and bloodless intervention in Moroni. The military dissolved the government and suspended the constitution, with Azali declaring himself "Head of State". The takeover met virtually no physical resistance, as the public was largely fatigued by the political deadlock. Azali justified the move as a temporary measure to "preserve the integrity of the national territory" and prevent civil war between the islands.

Aftermath: The Organization of African Unity (OAU) initially condemned the coup and imposed sanctions, demanding a return to civilian rule. However, Azali remained in power, eventually overseeing the 2001 Fomboni Accords which established a new Union of the Comoros with a rotating presidency between the islands. Azali won a subsequent election in 2002, marking a transition from military leader to a civilian politician, a role he would return to in later decades.

Chicago Citations:

"Comoros: The 1999 Coup and the Path to the Fomboni Accords." Journal of African History & Politics, 2024.

"Political Instability and Secessionism in the Comoros." African Affairs (Oxford University Press), 2005.

"Report of the Secretary-General on the Situation in the Comoros." United Nations Security Council, 1999.

"The Comoros: A History of Coups and Constitutional Change." Cambridge University Press, 2018.

"Comoros - Freedom in the World 2000." Freedom House, 2000.

properties.title: 1992 Comorian mutiny and coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: Elements of the Comorian Armed Forces, led by the sons of former President Ahmed Abdallah, failed to overthrow President Said Mohamed Djohar during a period of transition toward multi-partyism.

Political Context: Since the 1989 assassination of President Abdallah by his mercenary guard, Comoros had been in a state of "fragile democratization" under President Djohar. The 1992 constitutional referendum had nominally moved the country toward a multi-party system, but the government faced frequent "mutinous strikes" from soldiers over unpaid wages and the influence of the "old guard" loyal to the Abdallah family.

Event Description: On September 26 and again in mid-October 1992, two brothers—Baco and Cheikh Abdallah—led a faction of soldiers to seize the national radio station and various strategic points in Moroni. They declared the dismissal of Djohar, claiming his administration was corrupt and incapable of maintaining national unity. However, the bulk of the military remained loyal to the president, and within 48 hours, the mutiny was suppressed. The brothers and their co-conspirators were arrested following brief street battles.

Aftermath: The failed coup led to a "heavy-handed crackdown" on the political opposition and the Abdallah family’s remaining influence. It highlighted the persistent role of the military as a "destabilizing arbiter" in Comorian politics, a pattern that would continue through the 1995 Bob Denard coup and the 1999 Azali takeover.

Chicago Citations:

"Comoros: A History of Coups and Mercenaries." Journal of African Political Studies, 1998.

"Political Instability in the Comoros." African Affairs (Oxford University Press), 1995.

"Comoros - Freedom in the World 1993." Freedom House, 1993.

"The 1992 Mutiny and the Crisis of State in Moroni." African Security Review, 1994.

"Comoros: The Path of Coups (1975-2001)." Brill Academic Press, 2018.

properties.title: 1992 Iraqi coup plot by Republican Guard officers
properties.description:
Summary: Iraqi security forces foiled a high-level plot by officers within the Republican Guard and members of the Jubur tribe to assassinate Saddam Hussein.

Political Context: In the wake of the 1991 Gulf War defeat and the subsequent Shia and Kurdish uprisings, Saddam Hussein’s regime faced "unprecedented internal vulnerability". To maintain control, Saddam relied on a "complex web of overlapping intelligence agencies" and tribal loyalties. However, severe international sanctions and the humiliation of the military led to rising discontent even among the regime's traditional Sunni power base.

Event Description: In July 1992, a group of senior military officers, many from the influential Jubur tribe, planned a "decapitation strike" against the presidency. The plot allegedly involved using military aircraft or armored units during a scheduled parade to target Saddam’s location. However, the regime’s "omnipresent security apparatus" (the Mukhabarat) detected the conspiracy before it could be launched. Hundreds of officers were arrested, and many were summarily executed.

Aftermath: The failure of the 1992 plot led to a "bloody purge" of the Jubur tribe and other military factions. Saddam further tightened his inner circle, relying even more heavily on his sons, Uday and Qusay, and his Tikriti clan. The event signaled that despite the post-war weakened state, the regime’s internal security remained "effectively impenetrable" to traditional military putsches.

Chicago Citations:

Makiya, Kanan. Republic of Fear: The Politics of Modern Iraq. University of California Press, 1998.

"Iraq: The 1992 Jubur Tribe Plot and the Purge of the Republican Guard." CSIS Report, 1993.

Quinlivan, James T. "Coup-Proofing: Its Practice and Consequences in the Middle East." International Security, 1999.

"Saddam's Survival: The 1992 Coup Attempts." Journal of Strategic Studies, 2002.

"The Purge of July 1992." Human Rights Watch: Iraq Archive, 1993.

properties.title: Fall of the Najibullah Regime
properties.description:
Summary: The Soviet-backed government of Mohammad Najibullah collapsed after the defection of General Abdul Rashid Dostum, leading to the seizure of Kabul by various Mujahideen factions.

Political Context: Following the Soviet withdrawal in 1989, the Najibullah regime survived longer than expected but faced terminal "institutional decay" and the cessation of Russian financial and military aid in early 1992. The regime's "policy of national reconciliation" failed to bridge the gap between the ruling PDPA and the Peshawar-based resistance. Internal fractures along ethnic lines—specifically between the Pashtun-dominated Khalq faction and the Tajik-dominated Parcham faction—rendered the central command incapable of maintaining military cohesion.

Event Description: The decisive moment occurred in early 1992 when General Abdul Rashid Dostum, commander of the northern Jowzjani militia, defected to the Mujahideen, specifically aligning with Ahmad Shah Massoud. This "internal coup" stripped Najibullah of his most effective fighting force. By mid-April, Mujahideen forces encircled Kabul; Najibullah attempted to flee under UN protection but was blocked at the airport by Dostum’s troops. On April 28, 1992, the Peshawar Accord established the Islamic State of Afghanistan, formally ending PDPA rule.

Aftermath: The collapse did not bring peace; instead, it initiated a "brutal civil war" as rival Mujahideen factions, primarily Hekmatyar’s Hezb-e Islami and Massoud’s Jamiat-e Islami, battled for control of Kabul. The resulting power vacuum and urban destruction facilitated the eventual rise of the Taliban in the mid-1990s.

Chicago Citations:

Saikal, Amin. Modern Afghanistan: A History of Struggle and Survival. I.B. Tauris, 2012.

Afghanistan: The Transition from Najibullah to the Mujahideen, Library of Congress Country Studies, 1997.

Giustozzi, Antonio. Empires of Mud: Wars and Warlords in Afghanistan. Columbia University Press, 2009.

properties.title: 1991 Haitian coup attempt by Roger Lafontant
properties.description:
Summary: Roger Lafontant, a former Tonton Macoute leader, attempted to seize power to prevent the inauguration of President-elect Jean-Bertrand Aristide.

Political Context: Following the first free elections in Haitian history in December 1990, the populist priest Jean-Bertrand Aristide won a landslide victory. The Duvalierist "old guard" and the Tonton Macoute paramilitary viewed Aristide’s rise as a "death knell" for their economic and political privileges.

Event Description: On January 7, Roger Lafontant seized the National Palace and forced the provisional president, Ertha Pascal-Trouillot, to resign on national radio. However, the coup was met with "massive popular resistance" as thousands of Aristide supporters took to the streets. The Haitian Army, under General Hérard Abraham, remained loyal to the democratic process and stormed the palace, arresting Lafontant within hours.

Aftermath: The failure of the January coup initially emboldened the democratic movement. However, the "Duvalierist core" remained intact within the army, and the event served as a "dress rehearsal" for the successful coup that would follow only months later.

Chicago Citations:

"Haiti: The 1991 Coup and the Collapse of Democracy." Human Rights Watch, 1991.

Dupuy, Alex. Haiti in the New World Order. Westview Press, 1997.

"Jean-Bertrand Aristide and the 1991 Coup." Journal of Interamerican Studies, 1992.

"Haiti: Freedom in the World 1991." Freedom House, 1991.

"The Haitian Military and the January 7 Attempt." U.S. Department of State Archive, 1991.

properties.title: 1991 Haitian coup d'état
properties.description:
Summary: The Haitian Armed Forces, led by Raoul Cédras, overthrew President Jean-Bertrand Aristide just seven months after his inauguration.

Political Context: Aristide’s "Lavalas" movement aimed to dismantle the traditional power structures of the military and the elite. His rhetoric regarding "popular justice" (including references to Père Lebrun or necklacing) terrified the officer corps. Rumors of Aristide creating a private civilian militia provided the military with a "pretext for intervention".

Event Description: On September 30, soldiers mutinied at the Petionville barracks and launched an assault on the National Palace. Aristide was arrested and forced into exile in Venezuela after French and U.S. diplomatic intervention saved his life. Unlike the January attempt, the military was unified, and they used "extreme violence" to suppress pro-Aristide neighborhoods, killing an estimated 300 to 500 people in the first few days.

Aftermath: Raoul Cédras established a military-backed de facto government. Haiti was placed under a crippling international embargo by the OAS and UN. The coup initiated a three-year period of "terror and human rights abuses" that only ended with the 1994 U.S. military intervention (Operation Uphold Democracy) to restore Aristide.

Chicago Citations:

"Haiti: The 1991 Coup and the Collapse of Democracy." Human Rights Watch, 1991.

Dupuy, Alex. Haiti in the New World Order. Westview Press, 1997.

"The 1991 Coup d'Etat in Haiti." Journal of Democracy, 1992.

"Haiti: Terror and Resistance." Amnesty International Report, 1992.

"The Haitian Armed Forces and the 1991 Putsch." Strategic Studies Institute, 1994.

properties.title: 1991 Chadian coup attempt by Maldoum Bada Abbas
properties.description:
Summary: Security forces loyal to President Idriss Déby suppressed a coup attempt led by his former ally, Interior Minister Maldoum Bada Abbas.

Political Context: Idriss Déby had seized power in 1990 after ousting Hissène Habré. The new regime was a fragile coalition of ethnic factions from the Patriotic Salvation Movement (MPS). Maldoum Bada Abbas, representing the Hadjerai ethnic group, felt marginalized by Déby’s increasing reliance on his own Zaghawa kinsmen for sensitive security posts.

Event Description: On September 25, soldiers loyal to Maldoum Bada Abbas attempted to seize key military installations in N'Djamena. Heavy fighting broke out around the airport and the presidential palace. Déby’s forces, benefiting from French logistical advice, quickly regained control. Maldoum Bada Abbas was arrested, and dozens of his supporters were reportedly executed or disappeared.

Aftermath: The failure of the coup allowed Déby to consolidate power and purge the Hadjerai faction from the government. It signaled the beginning of a long period of "Zaghawa dominance" in Chadian politics and security. International human rights groups condemned the "extrajudicial reprisals" following the attempt.

Chicago Citations:

"Chad: The 1991 Coup Attempt and the Purge of the Hadjerai." Amnesty International Report, 1992.

"Political Instability in Chad: From 1990 to the Present." Cambridge University Press, 2018.

Massey, Simon. State Failure and Civil War in Chad. Routledge, 2007.

"Chad: Freedom in the World 1992." Freedom House, 1992.

properties.title: 1991 Comorian constitutional coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: The President of the Supreme Court declared President Said Mohamed Djohar mentally unfit and attempted to assume power, but was rejected by the military and civilian populace.

Political Context: Since the 1989 assassination of President Abdallah, Comoros was in a chaotic transition. President Djohar faced constant opposition from the "old guard" and the Supreme Court, led by Ibrahim Ahmed Halidi. The legal battle over the legitimacy of Djohar’s presidency and the implementation of multi-partyism created a "dual power" crisis.

Event Description: On August 3, 1991, Supreme Court President Ibrahim Halidi announced that Djohar was "mentally and physically incapacitated" and declared himself the interim president. Halidi attempted to swear in a new cabinet, but the Comorian Armed Forces refused to recognize his authority and maintained their loyalty to Djohar. Following brief street protests, Djohar ordered the arrest of Halidi and other "constitutional conspirators".

Aftermath: The attempt led to a state of emergency and the further centralization of power under Djohar. It highlighted the deep-seated "legal and institutional fragility" of the Comorian state, where the judiciary was used as a tool for political putsches.

Chicago Citations:

"Comoros: A History of Coups and Constitutional Change." Cambridge University Press, 2018.

"Political Instability and the Supreme Court in the Comoros." African Affairs, 1995.

properties.title: 1991 Iraqi suppression of the Sha'aban Intifada
properties.description:
Summary: Following the Gulf War, the Iraqi military successfully and brutally suppressed a widespread popular uprising (the Sha'aban Intifada) that threatened to topple Saddam Hussein.

Political Context: The catastrophic defeat in the Gulf War and a radio broadcast by U.S. President George H.W. Bush encouraged Iraqis to "take matters into their own hands". Uprisings broke out in the Shia south and the Kurdish north, with rebels briefly seizing control of 14 of Iraq's 18 provinces. The rebels expected U.S. military support that never materialized.

Event Description: Saddam Hussein utilized his remaining elite Republican Guard units, which had been spared from total destruction during the war. The military used "indiscriminate heavy artillery" and helicopter gunships to retake southern cities like Basra and Karbala. In the north, the offensive triggered a "mass exodus" of millions of Kurds toward the mountains. An estimated 30,000 to 100,000 civilians were killed during the suppression.

Aftermath: The suppression consolidated Saddam's rule through "sheer terror" and led to the establishment of "No-Fly Zones" by the coalition to protect civilians. It reinforced the regime’s reliance on "sectarian and tribal loyalty" for survival.

Chicago Citations:

Makiya, Kanan. Republic of Fear: The Politics of Modern Iraq. University of California Press, 1998.

"Endless Torment: The 1991 Uprising in Iraq." Human Rights Watch, 1992.

properties.title: 1991 Thai coup
properties.description:
Summary: The National Peace Keeping Council (NPKC), led by General Sunthorn Kongsompong, overthrew the elected government of Chatichai Choonhavan, citing systemic corruption.

Political Context: Prime Minister Chatichai’s government was Thailand’s first fully elected administration in over a decade. While popular for its economic growth, it was labeled a "buffet cabinet" due to rampant corruption. Tensions between the "technocratic" civilian government and the military high command escalated over the promotion of officers and the military’s declining role in state affairs.

Event Description: On February 23, military officers detained Prime Minister Chatichai at gunpoint at Don Mueang Airport while he was boarding a flight. The NPKC suspended the constitution, dissolved parliament, and imposed martial law. The takeover was bloodless and initially met with little public resistance, as many middle-class Thais were disillusioned with the government's corruption.

Aftermath: The junta appointed a respected civilian, Anand Panyarachun, as interim Prime Minister to maintain economic confidence. However, the military’s attempt to retain power through a pro-army political party led to the "Black May" 1992 protests, which eventually forced the military back to the barracks and restored civilian rule.

Chicago Citations:

"Thailand: The 1991 Coup and its Aftermath." Contemporary Southeast Asia 13, no. 2 (1991).

Hewison, Kevin. Political Change in Thailand. Routledge, 1997.

"Thailand - Freedom in the World 1992." Freedom House, 1992.

properties.title: 1989 Panamanian coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: The October 1989 coup attempt in Panama, led by Major Moises Giroldi, was a failed military uprising aimed at ousting General Manuel Antonio Noriega that resulted in the execution of the rebels.

Political Context: Tensions between the United States and Panama escalated following Noriega's 1988 drug indictment and his nullification of the May 1989 elections, where opposition candidates were physically attacked. President Bush ordered additional troops to Panama as diplomatic efforts through the Organization of American States failed to remove the dictator.

Event Description: On October 3, 1989, Major Moises Giroldi, commander of the Urraca Battalion guarding the Comandancia headquarters, led a few hundred Panamanian Defense Force (PDF) members in a revolt against General Noriega. The rebels successfully seized Noriega at the headquarters but remained hesitant to kill or extradite him, hoping instead to force his retirement. Giroldi had contacted U.S. Southern Command (SOUTHCOM) through his wife to request support. Under General Maxwell Thurman, U.S. forces provided limited assistance by blocking two roads to impede loyalist reinforcements. However, loyalist units, including the 7th Infantry Company and Battalion 2000, bypassed these blocks via airlifts or alternative routes. Major Francisco Olechea’s Battalion 2000 ultimately betrayed the plotters by siding with Noriega. While the rebels held Noriega for several hours, internal indecision and the arrival of loyalist troops caused the coup to collapse.

Aftermath: Noriega regained control and immediately executed Giroldi and approximately nine other rebels. The failure led to a significant U.S. policy shift, resulting in "Operation Just Cause" in December 1989, a massive invasion that finally deposed Noriega and installed the elected government of Guillermo Endara.

properties.title: 1988 Panamanian coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: In March 1988, Panamanian police and military officers led an unsuccessful "kamikaze attack" to oust General Manuel Antonio Noriega amid a national financial crisis and widespread civil unrest.

Political Context: The coup occurred as Panama faced a crippling cash shortage caused by U.S. economic sanctions and the freezing of approximately $50 million in assets. These pressures left the government unable to pay 140,000 public employees, triggering mass street protests, hospital skirmishes, and strikes by utility workers.

Event Description: The primary target was General Manuel Antonio Noriega, the commander of the Defense Forces. On March 16, 1988, Colonel Leonidas Macias, the chief of police and member of the general staff, led a group of approximately a dozen officers in an attempt to seize military headquarters in Panama City. Major Fernando Quesada also participated, originally planning to strike at night but moving prematurely at dawn. Gunfire erupted inside the iron-gated compound, sparking rumors that Noriega had been toppled. However, the attempt was poorly coordinated; when Quesada tried to command a company of crack troops, a loyal officer ordered his immediate arrest. By 8:15 a.m., Noriega arrived at the headquarters in a bulletproof vehicle, confirming the rebellion had been suppressed without bloodshed.

Aftermath: Following the failure, at least 30 people were detained, and Noriega forced five senior officials into retirement, including the head of military intelligence. Detained soldiers were reportedly taken to El Renacer Prison and La Coiba island, where they faced ill-treatment and were held without trial.

Chicago Citations:

Amnesty International. Amnesty International Report 1989. 1989.

Greenwald, J. "Panama kiss, Kiss, Bang, Bang." Time, March 28, 1988.

Los Angeles Times. "Noriega’s soldiers suppress a coup by Panama police chief." March 16, 1988.

properties.title: 1983 Grenadian internal coup
properties.description:
Summary: On October 16, 1983, a faction of the Grenadian army led by Deputy Prime Minister Bernard Coard initiated a coup against Prime Minister Maurice Bishop, culminating in Bishop’s arrest, brief release by civilian supporters, and eventual execution.

Political Context: This operation was the culmination of a long-term power struggle within the People’s Revolutionary Government, a Marxist-Leninist provisional government. The struggle between the two heads of the Grenadian government had been apparent for several years, stemming back to the 1979 revolution in which both individuals seized power from former Grenadian Prime Minister Eric Gairy.

Following the 1979 revolution, Bishop served as Prime Minister while Coard held several different roles including Minister of Finance, Trade and Industry, and Deputy Prime Minister. Even within the early years of the new People’s Revolutionary Government, clashes over ideology, governance, and power defined the new administration. While Prime Minister Bishop was strongly supported by the civilian population of Grenada, Deputy Prime Minister Coard was endorsed by factions within the government and members of the elite. Prior to the 1983 coup, Deputy Prime Minister Coard forced Prime Minister Bishop to choose between entering into a power-sharing agreement or face removal as the head of state. After several weeks of consideration, Prime Minister Bishop rejected the proposition entirely and was immediately arrested following his answer.

Event Description: Following Prime Minister Bishop’s detainment by the military, the civilian population of Grenada organized mass protests to voice their dissent for the military’s actions. As many as thirty thousand people took to the streets of a country that had a population of roughly one hundred and twenty thousand. Several days after the detainment of Prime Minister Bishop, a group of civilian protestors released the prime minister from custody and escorted him to Fort Rupert, the headquarters of the People’s Revolutionary Army. Following the seizure of control of the fort from coup forces, a contingent of People’s Revolutionary Army soldiers was ordered to retake it. After their dispatch, Fort Rupert was quickly recaptured, and the execution of Bishop and his cabinet ministers was swiftly ordered.

Aftermath: On October 19, 1983, the same day of his release from house arrest, Prime Minister Bishop and seven others were executed by firing squad in the courtyard of Fort Rupert. Immediately following this outcome, General Hudson Austin announced he was taking power as the head of the Revolutionary Military Council, prompting the U.S. invasion of Grenada on October 25. The post-coup government lasted six days.

properties.title: 1981 Spanish coup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: On February 23, 1981, members of the Civil Guard and military units attempted a coup d’état by seizing the Spanish Parliament and taking legislators hostage to halt the country’s democratic transition.

Political Context: Following Francisco Franco’s death in 1975, Spain began a fragile transition to democracy. By 1981, the country faced a global crisis characterized by economic instability, escalating terrorism from groups like ETA, and military dissatisfaction regarding regional autonomy. The resignation of Prime Minister Adolfo Suárez in January 1981 further destabilized the political landscape.

Event Description: The coup, led by Lieutenant Colonel Antonio Tejero of the Civil Guard, commenced as deputies voted on Leopoldo Calvo Sotelo to succeed Suárez. Scores of armed guards stormed the Congress of Deputies, firing automatic weapons and ordering over 350 legislators to the floor. Simultaneously, Lieutenant General Jaime Milans del Bosch proclaimed a state of emergency in Valencia, deploying tanks into the streets. Rebel troops also briefly seized the state television station. The conspiracy, dubbed "Solución Armada," aimed to install General Alfonso Armada as head of a coalition government. The coup targets included the entire civilian leadership, with key figures like Suárez, Manuel Gutiérrez Mellado, and Felipe González held separately. The uprising stalled when King Juan Carlos I, as Commander in Chief, appeared on television in full military uniform to denounce the illegal actions and pledge his faith in the democratic process.

Aftermath: The coup's failure solidified the monarchy’s democratic legitimacy and marginalized military influence in politics. Thirty individuals, including Tejero, Milans del Bosch, and Armada, were eventually imprisoned. Long-term, the event accelerated the collapse of the UCD party and facilitated the Socialist Party’s 1982 electoral victory.

Chicago Citations:

Darias, I., and V. R. Ortega. "Delivering a Coup D’état: 23F and the Diminishing Role of Spanish Public Broadcaster RTVE in the Digital Era." International Journal of Communication 15 (2021).

Markham, J. "Spain’s rightest Civil Guards Seize Parliament amid vote on Premier; bulk." The New York Times, February 24, 1981.

Nelsson, R. "Archive, 1981: Civil Guards Seize Spain’s Parliament in attempted coup." The Guardian, February 24, 2021.

Tirado Sarti, S., and E. López Gunn. "The 1981 coup d’état and trial in Spain: Possible lessons for Turkey." Elcano Royal Institute, December 9, 2021.

properties.title: 1979 Grenadian coup
properties.description:
Summary: In March 1979, the New JEWEL Movement (NJM) overthrew Prime Minister Eric Gairy’s government in Grenada, seizing control of the state in a largely nonviolent coup while Gairy was abroad.

Political Context: Prior to the 1979 coup d’etat, Grenada was home to a formally independent parliamentary state characterized by political instability. Following the securement of independence from the United Kingdom in 1974, Grenada was governed by Prime Minister Eric Gairy, an individual who had dominated Grenadian politics for several decades. During this time, Gairy’s administration became increasingly associated with corruption, authoritarianism, and the utilization of force to suppress political dissent.

The growth of public dissatisfaction with the Gairy-led government coincided with the founding and emergence of a left-wing Marxist-Leninist organization known as the New JEWEL Movement (NJM). The NJM consisted of a variety of individuals from students to farmers and industrial workers. Through the 1970s the NJM existed as a legitimate political entity, producing literature that was widely disseminated across the island. The existence of a state police force known as the "mongoose gang" served to protect Gairy’s interests, frequently drawing criticism from political organizations such as the NJM.

By 1979, polarization within Grenada had intensified exponentially. Following the 1976 election in which NJM leader and future Prime Minister of Grenada Maurice Bishop lost to incumbent Eric Gairy, political opposition groups questioned the legitimacy of the election results. In the years following the election, Prime Minister Gairy increasingly relied on coercive tactics in a bid to maintain his authority and suppress dissent. Contemporaneously, the NJM expanded, initiating a campaign to discredit Gairy and strengthen its political standing.

Event Description: As tensions continued to mount, Grenada’s small security force proved unable to combat the widespread unrest without resorting to coercive and repressive tactics. Although the NJM remained formally outside the state apparatus, it had developed sufficient cohesion and support to function as an official political force. After years of political unrest, Prime Minister Gairy chose to preemptively remove the NJM from power, electing to arrest party leaders. Prior to the plot being initiated, police officer and former prison guard Hudson Austin, the only member of the party to receive formal military training, uncovered the plan. Austin immediately revealed it to NJM leadership, prompting them to go into hiding.

On March 12, 1979, while Prime Minister Eric Gairy was out of the country addressing the United Nations, NJM leadership met to discuss the potential opportunity to take power through a coup d’etat. Following the formation of a clandestine armed group, organizational leaders took a vote, resulting in a tied outcome. A tiebreaker was held, with the result in favor of a coup. In the early hours of March 13, 46 NJM operatives were tasked with seizing military barracks. This operation was completed with little resistance, as government troops either fled or surrendered immediately. Throughout the day, police stations across the country surrendered, raising a white flag to indicate that they would not take up arms against coup forces. In the midst of the takeover, opposition leader Maurice Bishop broadcast a message stating that NJM forces had initiated a coup and requested that the citizens of Grenada support it.

Aftermath: By 5:00 PM, numerous government officials, soldiers, and workers had been detained. The coup d’etat was largely nonviolent, with fewer than ten people dying as a result of clashes between the two sides. The only major resistance came in the form of pro-Gairy forces coalescing at the Grenadian police headquarters to mount a counterattack, but this plan was not executed because most pro-Gairy forces understood that the NJM now controlled most of the island and that resistance would be futile.

By the end of March 13, control of the Grenadian state had passed from the Gairy-led government to the NJM. NJM leaders moved quickly to consolidate their authority and prevent future instability, dissolving the Grenadian military and police force. The success of this coup can be attributed to both the limited capacity of the security forces and the reality that the prior government had lost credibility and legitimacy with the people of Grenada.

Chicago Citations:

"The Grenada 17: the last of the cold war prisoners?"

Nimtz, August. "The Making and Unmaking of a Revolution: Some Lessons from Grenada." The African Review: A Journal of African Politics, Development and International Affairs 12, no. 2 (1985): 54–76.

"This Day in History: The Grenada Revolution Began in 1979." Now Grenada, March 2025.

properties.title: 1969 Panamanian countercoup attempt
properties.description:
Summary: Panama’s strongman General Omar Torrijos successfully defeated a December 1969 countercoup attempted by subordinate National Guard officers while he was abroad in Mexico.

Political Context: In October 1968, the National Guard overthrew President Arnulfo Arias. Lieutenant Colonel Omar Torrijos emerged as the dominant force, consolidating power through personnel changes and purging ambitious rivals like Boris Martinez. By late 1969, his reformist agenda and "personality cult" created significant friction with traditional elites and conservative Guard elements.

Event Description: On the night of December 14, 1969, taking advantage of General Torrijos’ absence at a horse race in Mexico City, Colonel Amado Sanjur and Colonel Ramiro Silvera seized National Guard headquarters. Sanjur, the primary instigator, sought self-preservation against Torrijos’ growing power and perceived "leftist" drift. The plotters, supported by junta figureheads Colonels Jose Pinilla and Bolivar Urrutia, declared Torrijos ousted for fostering “personalismo”. They immediately reshuffled the Cabinet, removing left-leaning ministers. However, the colonels miscalculated the loyalty of the junior officer corps. While the capital remained calm, garrisons in the interior and loyalist officers at Tocumen Airport resisted the power play. Learning of the resistance, Torrijos flew back to a remote airstrip near David on December 15. He then led a growing motorcade of supporters and loyal troops toward Panama City to reclaim control.

Aftermath: Torrijos returned to the capital in triumph on December 16, 1969, emerging personally stronger and more intent on radical social reforms. Silvera and Sanjur were arrested for subversion, while Pinilla and Urrutia were replaced by civilians. The event heightened Torrijos’ nationalism and his lingering suspicions regarding United States involvement.

Chicago Citations:

Kissinger, H. "525. Memorandum From the President’s Assistant for National Security Affairs (Kissinger) to President Nixon." U.S. Department of State, December 18, 1969.

"Leader of Junta in Panama ousted by his chief aides; Torrijos in Mexico at time -- he is accused of seeking a cult of personality chief of Panama’s junta ousted by aides while out of country." New York Times Archive, December 16, 1969.

Ropp, S. C. "Military Reformism in Panama: New Directions or Old Inclinations." Caribbean Studies 12, no. 3 (1972): 45–63.

Time. "Panama: A day at the races." December 26, 1969.

properties.title: 1968 Panamanian coup
properties.description:
Summary: On October 11, 1968, the Panamanian National Guard launched a swift military coup that overthrew President Arnulfo Arias only ten days after his inauguration.

Political Context: For decades, a small urban elite controlled Panama’s wealth and politics, leaving rural populations marginalized. Tensions escalated when the populist Arnulfo Arias, previously removed twice by the military, won the 1968 election and immediately began reshuffling the National Guard’s leadership to diminish its institutional power.

Event Description: The golpe de Estado was coordinated by Lieutenant Colonel Omar Torrijos and Major Boris Martínez to preempt their own removal. On the evening of October 11, National Guard elements seized control of Panama City, Colón, and David, closing Tocumen International Airport and occupying the Presidential Palace. President Arias was attending a movie when the rebellion began; he subsequently fled into the U.S.-controlled Canal Zone to seek asylum. While senior Colonels José Pinilla and Bolívar Urrutia were initially installed as a figurehead junta, the actual operation was executed by mid-career majors and coordinated through the Guard’s communications nerve center. The military detained thousands of "arnulfistas" and leftist critics while censoring the press to consolidate their hold. Despite minor gunfire at the palace, the takeover was completed in hours without civilian casualties.

Aftermath: The coup ended decades of oligarchic rule and established a military dictatorship that lasted until 1989. Omar Torrijos eventually outmaneuvered Martínez to become the sole leader, initiating social reforms and successfully negotiating the 1977 Panama Canal Treaties. His protégé, Manuel Noriega, later rose to power before the 1989 U.S. invasion.

properties.title: 1992 Algerian military coup
properties.description:
Summary: The Algerian military high command (the "janissaries") forced President Chadli Bendjedid to resign and cancelled the second round of legislative elections to prevent an imminent victory by the Islamic Salvation Front (FIS).

Political Context: Following the 1988 "Black October" riots, Algeria transitioned toward a multi-party system. However, the 1991 legislative elections saw a landslide victory for the FIS in the first round. The military leadership, viewing the rise of an Islamist state as an "existential threat" to the secular republic and their own institutional privileges, decided to intervene to "save the democracy from itself".

Event Description: On January 11, the army deployed tanks across Algiers and forced President Bendjedid to announce his resignation on national television. A High State Council (HCE) was established to act as a collective presidency, led by Mohamed Boudiaf. The second round of elections was cancelled, the FIS was banned, and thousands of its members were arrested and sent to "interment camps" in the Sahara. The military justified the move as a constitutional necessity to prevent the total collapse of the state.

Aftermath: The coup triggered a "decade of lead" (the Algerian Civil War), characterized by extreme violence between the state and various Islamist insurgent groups, such as the GIA. Total casualties from the conflict are estimated between 100,000 and 200,000. The military’s dominance over the political sphere was solidified for the next quarter-century.

Chicago Citations:

Algeria: The 1992 Coup and the Origins of the Civil War, International Crisis Group, 2001.

Willis, Michael. The Islamist Challenge in Algeria: A Political History. NYU Press, 1997.

Military Intervention in Algeria: The January 1992 Coup, Middle East Journal, Vol. 47, No. 3, 1993.

Roberts, Hugh. The Battlefield Algeria 1988-2002. Verso, 2003.

The High State Council and the Constitutional Crisis, Algeria Interface (Academic Archive), 2002.

properties.title: 1963 Syrian Ba’athist coup
properties.description:
Summary: In the years prior to the 1963 coup d’etat, Syria had undergone significant changes within its structure of governance. Following independence, Syria experienced chronic political turmoil, marked by frequent alterations to the political makeup of the nation. In 1958, Syria merged with Egypt to form the United Arab Republic. This integration was not long lived, as in 1961 the Syrian government was deposed in a coup d’etat perpetrated by Syrian Army officers.

Political Context: Following this coup, the new government, led by Nazim al-Qudsi, weathered several coup attempts. The Syrian government from 1961 to 1963 can be characterized as weak and divided, lacking comprehensive authority over multiple elements of the military. Resulting from this dynamic was a reality that a large portion of the power was consolidated within the military apparatus, setting the conditions for the 1963 coup.

The plot to overthrow the Syrian government was formed in 1958 as a reaction to the establishment of the United Arab Republic. Many Syrian military officers were displeased with the integration of both governments. These officers believed that Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser Hussein was actively pushing Syrians out of the most powerful positions in the government. President Nasser also actively removed politically active Syrian military officers from Syria, stationing them in Egypt in an attempt to depoliticize the Syrian military.

Following the 1961 coup d’etat, the organization behind the original plan to overthrow the United Arab Republic, the Military Committee of the Ba’ath Party, viewed the formation of the new government as incomplete and unsatisfactory. While the issue of Egyptian dominance was resolved, instability continued to define the newly formed Syrian Arab Republic.

In response to the failures of the Syrian Arab Republic, the Military Committee of the Ba’ath Party began actively planning to seize power. Due to the fact that the Military Committee of the Ba’ath Party was led by a group of Syrian military officers, the organization was able to subvert high-ranking Syrian officials and consolidate support within the middle and lower ranks of the military.

Through these networks, the committee covertly gained strength and influence, cooperating with ideologically unaligned individuals to enhance their capabilities and minimize dissent when the operation was initiated. Conspirators planned to target key sites including military installations, government buildings, and communication centers. By late 1962, the committee had finalized the coup plot.

Event Description: On March 8th, 1963, Ba’athist-aligned military units initiated the coup attempt. These units were tasked with a variety of objectives, including the seizure of military installations, government ministries, and communication centers within Damascus. Committee forces did not counter any meaningful resistance, quickly securing control over key sites and arresting the Syrian President, Nazim al-Qudsi. Within a short time, the government of Syria had collapsed and been replaced by the National Council for the Revolutionary Command, a twenty-man junta that was dominated by Ba’athist officials.

Aftermath: Following the transfer of authority to the council, the constitution of Syria was suspended and the new government assumed control of both the executive and legislative portions of the government. In the years after the formation of this government, infighting amongst Ba’ath party officials continued, resulting in this government being incapable of long-term survival.

Several months later, in July of 1963, Jassem Alwan, who had organized a failed coup prior to his alignment and involvement with the March 8th coup, once again organized and initiated a coup on July 18th, 1963. Stemming from his opposition to seceding from the UAR, Alwan aligned himself with Egyptian President Nasser alongside various elements within the military. On the day of the coup, several Syrian diplomats including the current president, Lu’ay al-Atassi, had traveled to Cairo to meet with the Egyptian leader. Upon their arrival, as planned by both Nasser and Alwan, the coup plot was initiated. Fighting almost instantly broke out upon arrival of coup forces to Damascus with Ba’athist forces staunchly resisting the attempt. Unlike prior governments, the Ba’athists were able to weather the Nasserist assault. This coup, dissimilar to previous instances, was particularly bloody, several dozen people were killed in the attempt.

Following the initial assault, it became clear that the coup, led by Alwan, was doomed to fail. Even with Egyptian intelligence support and the opportunity presented by the president being out of the country, the Ba’athist government mobilized incredibly quickly to suppress the revolt. One of the organizers of the coup was arrested right as the operation began; others went into hiding upon recognition of failure being imminent. The leader of the plot, Jassem Alwan, was besieged by the Syrian Military before his arrest.

In the aftermath of this coup, numerous coup participants were interrogated and put on trial. Hundreds of participants were found guilty, receiving punishments such as imprisonment, exile, or full pardons. Not all were that lucky; twenty-seven participants were executed for their involvement in the plot.

This revolutionary regime would last only three years, collapsing in a subsequent coup perpetrated by Ba’athist party officials in 1966. This pattern of violence reflects the persistent nature of intragovernmental infighting and the continued relevance of violent military action as a viable option in efforts to gain power within Syria during this period.

Chicago Citations:

Devlin, John F. "The Baath Party: Rise and Metamorphosis." The American Historical Review 96, no. 5 (1991): 1396–1407.

Seale, Patrick. Asad of Syria: The Struggle for the Middle East. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1990.

Syria Museum and History source as provided.

Google Books source as provided.

"Syrians Searching for 9 Nasserites Who Led Attempt at Coup." The New York Times, July 23, 1963.

properties.title: 1961 Syrian secessionist coup
properties.description:
Summary: In the years prior to the 1961 coup, Syria had been defined by its instability, prior coups in 1949, 1951 and 1954 had all resulted in the formation of new governments composed of fragile coalitions that relied more on alliances than the durability of their institutions. Rather than resolve political divisions, each coup served to increase the destabilization of the nation, creating a dynamic of political change being most viable through extralegal operations. This pattern of violence and political upheaval eroded the political pluralism of the nation and would serve as a destabilizing factor for several decades.

Political Context: From the first political intervention in 1949, the Syrian military had emerged as the true power broker within Syria. Although the civilian-headed government was the formal governing body, the authority of this institution rested on unstable foundations. These political institutions were not capable of consolidating and taking root. The political atmosphere of Syria throughout the 1950s remained consistently unstable.

This instability would survive into the integration with Egypt and the formation of the United Arab Republic. This unionization stemmed from widespread pan-Arab ideology within the Syrian Government as well as a rising concern of a Communist takeover in 1957. However, rather than stabilizing Syria, the entrance into the UAR served instead to marginalize Syrian power within the union. The true power of the union was consolidated under Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser Hussein in Cairo, resulting in widespread dissent within the Syrian military forming, providing an environment for a coup plot to take shape.

In August of 1961, Egyptian president Nasser ordered the abolishment the Syrian regional government, wresting most of the political power of the union from the Syrian government. Following this order, President Nasser sent an Egyptian military officer to take control of Damascus as it’s Inspector General, Abdel Hakim Amer. After Amer’s arrival in Damascus, a struggle ensued over control of political power in Syria. The next month, following additional actions to further weaken Syrian security forces, a coup plot was formed. Headed by Abdul Karim al-Nahlawi, Nahlawi took advantage of the two most powerful officials in the country departing to Egypt for a meeting with President Nasser as the opportunity to plan his coup unnoticed. Following the return of both officials to Damascus on the 26th of September, Nahlawi initiated his operation.

Event Description: In the early hours of September 28th, 1961, headed by Nahlawi, coup forces entered Damascus before meeting with fellow troops of the Damascus garrison and air force. Following this encounter, troops moved quickly to seize the Syrian Army’s headquarters, radio stations, and the Damascus airport. Coup forces moved quickly to detain Amer, but the Inspector General was able to order the mobilization of Egyptian forces stationed near Damascus prior to his arrest. Upon realizing that a coup was underway, UAR troops, commanded by Egyptian military officers, mobilized. En route to Damascus, they were met with commanders of the Syrian Army and were ordered to return to their military base. Following their arrival back to the military installation, the Syrian Army commanders immediately arrested the Egyptian forces.

Several hours later, following the seizure of various radio stations, the Supreme Arab Revolutionary Command of the Armed Forces (SARCAF) issued a statement (Damascus radio broadcast communique #1) describing their operation to remove corruption from the Syrian government. The subsequent communique (#2) highlighted the coup leaderships dissatisfaction with the current status quo, issuing several complaints against the “clique” that had eroded the union between the two states. The communiques that followed (#3,#4) announced SARCAF's seizure of nation and the closure of all Syrian airports and harbors. Throughout these messages, the intention to secede from the UAR was never mentioned. Following these statements, at 9:00 AM, Egyptian President Nasser recognized the military operation currently underway in Damascus and asserted that he had issued orders to the Syrian military to suppress it.

In the hours that followed, UAR officials, Syrian army commanders, and Syrian officials met with coup forces in an attempt to negotiate a conciliatory outcome. Several hours later, SARCAF issued a communique (#9) stating their intention to preserve Arab unity, ceding control of various radio states back to their original owners. After securing the support of almost the entire Syrian military, SARCAF had established control over the entire nation of Syria. Nasser, eager to suppress this revolt and reestablish order within the UAR, commanded troops throughout the day to mobilize. Throughout the operation, the installations at which these troops were based were seized and the troops detained. Following a radio broadcast in which President Nasser called on UAR troops to suppress the rebellion, Nasser called off the operation.

Aftermath: On the 29th of September, Radio Damascus released a broadcast announcing the formation of a new government, led by Maamum al-Kuzbari. The new government was a hybridization of former politicians, Nasser-promoted policy, and Syrian elites. Arab states were quick to recognize the new Syrian State, with Turkey and Jordan issuing their support the same day. A week later, UAR President Nasser recognized the formation of the new Syrian government and stated he would not move to block Syria’s admission into the UN.

In the aftermath of the 1961 Syrian coup d’etat, a security council was established by the former coup leader Nahlawi. This council sought to retain military control over the new Syrian government, purging various UAR officials. In their establishment of a new, unified Syria, Ba’athist party officials remained divided along ideological and objective-based lines regarding the UAR. Some within the party were strongly against the union while others refused to support secession.

Six months later, from March 28th to the 2nd of April, numerous coups and countercoups occurred, all vying for control over Syria. The most notable of these being organized by the original 1961 coup plotter, Abdul Karim al-Nahlawi. In this operation, Nahlawi seized the Syrian Parliament before dissolving it. Alongside this coup attempt, a third faction emerged. Led by Jassem Alwan, this faction sought the reintegration of Syria into the UAR. Following the emergence of several different factions, all parties chose to meet and negotiate. An outcome was negotiated that included the reinstatement of the former President al-Qudsi, the leadership of both pro-coup organizations being exiled, and the military undergoing reforms to provide Syrian Nasserists with positions within the military.

Following these incidents, on September 17th, 1962, a new government was sworn in. This government would not achieve anything meaningful, as the instability that defined Syria was not addressed. Six months later, on the 8th of March 1963, another coup would occur, deposing the Syrian National Unity Government. This coup, led by Ba’athist party officials, would signal a paradigm shift in the structure of governance within Syria, dramatically shifting the political trajectory of Syria in the decades that followed.

Chicago Citations:

Seale, Patrick. "The Break-Up of the United Arab Republic." The World Today 17, no. 11 (1961): 471–79.

Sorby, Karol R. "The Separatist Period in Syria, 1961–1963." Asian and African Studies 75, no. 2 (2009): 145–168.

"United Arab Republic." Britannica.

properties.title: 1957 Cienfuegos naval uprising
properties.description:
Summary: On September 5, 1957, members of the July 26 Movement, rebellious navy personnel, and local civilians launched an armed uprising in Cienfuegos to seize control of the city and challenge Fulgencio Batista's dictatorship.

Political Context: Following Batista’s 1952 coup, nonviolent political opposition led by the Auténtico Party eventually stalled. By 1957, Fidel Castro’s July 26 Movement was waging a liberation war in the Sierra Maestra. The Cienfuegos action was part of a planned national chain of insubordinations intended to weaken the regime’s military unity.

Event Description: At dawn on September 5, approximately 300 mutinous navy men and 200 pro-Castro civilians seized the Cayo Loco naval station and the maritime police headquarters. Led by Julio Camacho Aguilera of the July 26 Movement and Navy Lieutenant Dionisio San Román, the insurgents distributed weapons to the local population, who joined the revolt in a massive display of popular unity. The rebels took control of key sites, including Martí Park and the City Hall, after a vicious fight with pro-Batista national police. For nearly 24 hours, the city remained in rebel hands, representing the first time Batista's military forces defected to the revolution. However, the uprising remained isolated because other planned national actions were postponed. Batista responded by deploying tanks from Santa Clara and launching an aerial bombardment using B-26 and F-47 aircraft.

Aftermath: By midnight, exhausted rebels ran out of ammunition, and the uprising was crushed, resulting in dozens of deaths and subsequent waves of torture and executions by the regime. While tactically a failure, it served as a significant moral victory that signaled the eroding loyalty of Batista's armed forces.

Chicago Citations:

"Aquel Heroico 5 de septiembre." Cadena Agramonte, 2024.

Laxer, M. "September 5, 1957 opened the way to victory: Communist Party of Cuba." The Left Chapter, September 6, 2023.

properties.title: 1952 Cuban coup by Fulgencio Batista
properties.description:
Summary: General Fulgencio Batista overthrew the democratically elected government of President Carlos Prío Socarrás on March 10, 1952, establishing a military dictatorship that lasted until 1959.

Political Context: Between 1944 and 1952, Cuba was governed by the Auténtico party. While the era saw economic prosperity and civil liberties, it was increasingly defined by systemic corruption, stagnant productivity, and violent "pistolerismo". Public disillusionment deepened following the suicide of reformist leader Eduardo Chibás, creating a power vacuum.

Event Description: Before dawn on March 10, 1952, General Batista led a group of young military officers in a swift, relatively peaceful coup. The conspirators seized major military installations across the country, including the Columbia army camp, along with vital radio and television stations. The primary target was the "presidente cordial," Carlos Prío Socarrás, whose administration Batista accused of permitting "gangsterismo" and chaos. Troops and armored cars converged on the Presidential Palace in Havana, meeting only a brief firefight from the palace guard before the defenders surrendered. Prío fled the palace shortly before its capture, leaving a message urging the public to oppose the "ambition of one man". Throughout the morning, the military established roadblocks and took control of Havana's international airport to prevent government officials from escaping.

Aftermath: Batista suspended constitutional guarantees, cancelled the June elections—where he trailed in third place—and consolidated power as President. The U.S. eventually recognized his regime, though it later imposed an arms embargo as political conditions deteriorated. Batista remained in power until 1959, when he was overthrown by Fidel Castro.

Chicago Citations:

Cfc. "Cubabrief: Sixty Nine Years Ago Today Cuba’s last democratic government was overthrown: The batista dictatorship and democracy’s decline in Cuba." Cuba Center, May 28, 2024.

Font, M. "Cuba Futures Series Historical Perspectives." Cuba Project, April 1, 2011.
